subject: Geography
q:
(a) Distinguish between
(i) Latitude and Longitude
(ii) Standard Time and Greenwich Mean Time

(b) If the Greenwich Mean Time is 10:00am, 5th July when Local Time in a place X is 4:48am, 4th July, find the longitude of place X.
explanation:
(a)(i) Latitude is the angular distance of a point on the Earth's surface, measured in degrees from the centre of the earth. Lines of Latitude run from West to East and they can be used to calculate distance between one place to another. It extends between 90°N and 90°S. Longitudes are imaginery lines running from the North to the South at right angle to the parallel. The longitude that passes through the centre of the earth is called the prime or Greenwich Meridian. Longitude can be used to calculate local time and it extends between 180°W and 180°E.

(ii) Standard Time; is the time generally adopted by a particular country which is usually taken from the central meridian of that country. The need is to eliminate difference in local time between one town and another; otherwise it will result in changing time from one town to another. E.g Nigeria has accepted the meridian of 15°E for her standard time which is 1 hour ahead of the Greenwich Mean Time: Greenwich Mean Time is the time along the prime or Greenwich Meridian which is longitude 0°. It is from the GMT that all countries take their time E.g London and Accra are on longitude 0° and all other countries take their time from the GMT from London. If the GMT in London is 3.00pm, Nigerian time will be 4.00pm.

 (ii) Find the time difference
The time difference from 10.00am 5th July to 4.48am 4th July = 29 hours 12 mins.
29 hours 12 mins - 24 hours (one day) = 5hrs 12 minutes

(iii) Convert time difference (5 hrs 12 minutes)to longitude
15° = 1 hour
5 hrs = 15° x 5 =75°
4 minutes = 1°
12 mins.   12  = 3° 
                 4
 Total degree = 75° + 3° = 78°

(iv) Since X° is west of 0°, the longitude therefore is 78°W

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Define the term sedimentary rock and describe any two processes of its formation

(b) Discuss any three ways in which sedimentary rocks are important to man
explanation:
(a) Sedimentary rock is the type of rock formed from the accumulation of layers of deposits of sediments in water or on land. These sediments later join or cement together in layers to form sedimentary rock.

Two processes of sedimentary rock formation are:

(i) Mechanical Process: Involves the formation of sedimentary rock from sediments accumulated from other rocks such as igneous and metamorphic rocks. These  rocks settled in layers to form sedimentary rock E., J, formation of sandstone, shale, conglomerate.

(ii) Chemical Process: Pre-existing igneous and metamorphic rocks are precipitated chemically from solution of one kind or the other. Rock salt are derived from strata which once formed the beds of seas or lakes. Examples of rocks formed by chemical process include sodium chloride, gypsum, etc.

(b) Three ways in which sedimentary rocks are important to man are:

(i) Some sedimentary rocks are sources of some useful minerals such as coal and petroleum which serve as fuel energy to man.

(ii) Some sedimentary rocks are used for road construction and for building houses.

(iii) Rock salt, e.g sodium chloride (table salt) is a source of body mineral needed for maintaining osmotic pressure in the blood.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Identify and explain any four factors which determine the direction of flow of ocean current

(b) Using specific examples, discuss the effects of ocean currents on the climate of the adjacent coastal area.
explanation:
Four factors which determine the direction of flow of ocean current are
(i) Planetary winds
(ii) Temperature
(iii) Salinity
(iv) The Earth's rotation

(i) Planetary Winds: Between the equator and the tropics, the Trade Winds move equatorial water polewards and westwards and warm the eastern coasts of continents, e.g the North - East Trade winds move the North Equatorial current, Florida current and Gulf stream Drift, to warm the Southern and Eastern coasts of U.S.A.

(ii) Temperature: Because of the difference in temperature of ocean waters at the equator and at the poles the warm water is,lighter and rises and the cold water is denser and sinks hence warm equatorial water moves slowly along the surface poleward and the cold water moves equatorwards.

(iii) Salinity: Water of high salinity are denser than water of low salinity hence water of low salinity flows on the surface of water of high salinity

(iv) Rotation of the earth: The earth rotates from west to east and causes the ocean water to deflect to the right thereby making ocean water to deflect in a clockwise direction in the Northern hemisphere and anticlockwise in the Southern hemisphere.

(b) Effects of ocean current on the climate of the adjacent coastal areas,

(i) Warm current raise the temperatures of adjacent lands

(ii) Warm current brings regular and heavy rainfall to the coastlands

(iii) Warm current helps to keep ports in polar region free from ice

(iv) Cold current causes aridity and desert e.g Benguela current of west coast of South Africa causes Namid desert

(v) The meeting of two different current (i.e cold and warm currents) is a blessing as it results in the growth of planktons where fishing can be done.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Draw a labelled diagram of a simple soil profile

(b) Describe the characteristics of any two of the zones in the soil profile drawn in (a) above. Give reasons for each characteristic
explanation:
A Horizon: This horizon is also called the top soil. It contains remains of plants and animals which are undergoing decomposition to form humus. It is also known as the alluvial horizon or zone of eluviation which is impoverished by the washing of clay and fine soil particles down to the soil profit. It is the horizon with maximum biological activities of plants and soil micro-organisms.

B — Horizon: This horizon is also called the sub soil. It is the zone of enrichment (alluvial horizon or zone of illuviation). It is the accumulation of soil minerals, in which humus and other organic matter washed down from the A -horizon are converted into inorganic compound. '.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Differentiate between permanent drought and seasonal drought, giving an example of each

(b) Highlight any two main causes and two effects of drought

(c) Suggest any two methods of minimizing the effects of drought.
explanation:
(a) Permanent drought is the complete absence of precipitation in an area. There is virtually no water in the soil. This is common in deserts. Seasonal drought is the type in which an area only experiences drought at a particular season and plenty of rainfall or precipitation at the other season. E.g Some areas could have rainfall during the rainy season while they will experience dryness during the dry season. This situation results in seasonal drought.

(b)(i) Two causes of drought are; 

(i) Absence of precipitation or rainfall

(ii) Shifting of climatic zones which results in southwards creeping of the Sahara Desert.

(ii) Two effects of drought are; 

(i) Drought gives rise to large scale disruption of socio-economic life due to crop failure, lack of water and livestock pasture

(ii) It can lead to displacement of settlement and migration of people from drought—affected areas to other areas.

(c) Two methods of minimizing the effects of drought are:

(i) The application of large scale irrigation to water the area so as to stimulate the growth of vegetation

(ii) Afforestation: the planting of trees minimizes the level of drought as this could even result in the formation of rain.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Explain how each of the following has affected world population:
(i) Improved medical care
(ii) Advanced technology and industry

(b) Highlight four factors which account for the high density population in Java
explanation:
(a)(i) How improved medical care affected world population:- It has helped to increase population through

(i) knowledge of the causes of infectious diseases and how to prevent them

(ii) Provision of improved health service through availability of well-trained medical personnel

(iii) Knowledge of better sanitation

(iv) Reduced infant mortality rate as a result of better care.

(a)(ii) How advanced technology and industry affected world population. It has led to increased population through:

(i) Modernization of Agriculture, leading to plentiful and varied food supply

(ii) The use of fertilizers from industries ensures the availability of food hence increase in population.

(iii) Provision of storage and processing - facilities ensure the availability of food (either raw or processed form) all year round

(iv) Knowledge of prevention ar control of pests and diseases which helps to increase food production

(v) Provision of consumable goods from industries which ensure high standard of living.

(b) Four factors which account for the high density of population in Java are:

(i) Presence of rich volcanic soil (i.e tr soil is very fertile)

(ii) Abundance of rainfall which ensures smooth agricultural activities

(iii) Intensive agricultural activities attract people to the area (they grow 2-3 crops in a year)

(iv) Favourable climate conducive for intensive farming

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Name any two types of urban settlements

(b) Outline the characteristic features of each type

(c) Describe any four problems facing urban centres.
explanation:
(a) Two types of urban settlements are:
(i) Conurbation and
(ii) Megalopolis

(b) Characteristics of conurbation:
(i) It is made up of two or more towns
(ii) The towns are joined together
(iii) towns still retain their identity

Characteristics of megalopolis:
(i) It is made up of many cities
(iii) The cities a joined together
(iii) It represents the largest urban settlement

(c) Four problems facing urban centres are
(i) Inadequate transport facilities as a result of increasing number of people trooping into urban centres
(ii) Over-use and deterioration of available social amenities such as water, electrical school, etc
(iii) Increase in the rate of crime as a result of lack of employment of able-bodied persons
(iv) The problem of undue increase in human and vehicular traffic in urban areas.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Describe three advantages and three limitations of water transportation

(b) Explain how the limitations identified in (a) above can be overcome
explanation:
(a) Three advantages of water transportation are:

(i) Water transportation is the cheapest means of moving people and goods from one place to another:

(ii) Large quantities of goods can be carried at a time over a long distance

(ii) Oceans are free for use by all nations as there are no track to maintain except when approaching par

Three limitations of water transportation are:

(i) It is very slow

(ii) Prevalence of rapids and waterfalls make inland water transportation very difficult

(iii) Fluctuations in the volume of water in inland waterways makes navigation a big problem.

(b) Three ways in which the limitations identified in (a) can be overcome include

(i) Building of modern ships wit greater speed

(ii) Efforts should be made to eliminate the cataracts and rapids along inland navigation to ensure smooth movement of ships

(iii) Dredging of some important inland navigation water-ways should be done in order to ensure deep and large volume of water in these rivers.

subject: Geography
q: Give reasons for the high volume of trade between Nigeria and Japan
explanation:
(a) Reasons for high volume of trade between Nigeria and Japan include

(i) Goods produced by both countries ar different hence the increase in the volume of trade

(ii) Differences in climatic conditions create the conditions necessary for the production of different agricultural goods

(iii) Differences in the level of technology

(iv) Increase foreign exchange earnings

(v) Increase in the prices of goods offered for sale.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Name three broad classes into which manufacturing industries can be grouped and one important area in the world where an example of each is located

(b) Describe three main characteristics of each class of industry named in (a) above.
explanation:
(a) Three broad classes of manufacturing industries are

(i) Cottage or craft industries e.g West Africa

(ii) Light industries e.g India

(iii) Heavy industries e.g USA.

(b)(i) Three main characteristics of cottage or craft industry

(i) It involves very few people working at home

(ii) It not require power-driven machines

(iii) Special skills are required.

(bii) Three characteristics of light industries

(i) They produce relatively small light weigh goods

(ii) Products are mainly final or consumer goods

(iii) They usually employ a lot of female workers

(biii) Three characteristics of heavy industries

(i) They produce heavy and bulky goods

(ii) Products are neither final nor consumer goods but are used in construction or manufacturing industries

(iii) they usually employ male workers due to the nature of work involved.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) On an outline map of Nigeria, locate and name
(i) the four climatic belts
(ii) one important town in each belt

(b) Describe the seasonal pattern of rainfall in either southern or northern Nigeria

(c) Explain the effects of the seasonal pattern of rainfall on farming activities in the area chosen in (b) above.
explanation:
(b) Seasonal pattern of rainfall in Southern Nigeria; 

(i) The long wet season - Rainfall starts in March till July when plants look green due to heavy rains and high humidity

(ii) The short dry season - Called the August break where there is little or no rainfall in the month of August

(iii) The short wet season- Starts from August ending October. The rainfall is not heavy as the first season

(iv) The long dry, or harmattan season -Starts around November till mid-March with cool and misty morning, hazy afternoon and a cold and dry evening. There is virtually no rainfall during this time.

(c) The effects of seasonal pattern of rainfall on farming activities include

(i) it enables farmers to grow crops in a year

(ii) it enables farmers to grow tree crops because of heavy rainfall

(iii) Harvesting of crops is usually done especially during the dry season

(iv) Heavy rainfall however, discourages livestock farming because it does not favour grass growth.

q:
Write a brief account of fishing in Nigeria under the following headings:
(a) areas
(b) methods
(c) types of fish
(d) importance
explanation:
(a) Areas of fishing in Nigeria include the following:

(i) Inland fishing areas like rivers, Lake Chad and streams

(ii) Fishing in creeks and lagoons

(iii) Ocean or off-shore fishing

(b) Methods of fishing include:

(i) The use of traps

(ii) The use of hooks and lines

(ii) The use of nets

(iv) The use of trawlers in ocean fishing

(c) Types of fish include: Tilapia, Catfish, Claris, Shark, Mackerel, etc.

(d) Importance of fishing in Nigeria:

(i) Provision of food, it is a source of protein

(ii) It provides employment to many people

(iii) Fish exported can provide foreign exchange

(iv) Fish by-products are used as fish meal in livestock farming

(v) Establishment of fish processing industries.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) (i) On an outline map of Nigeria, show the area that is mostly affected by desert encroachment
(ii) Locate an name two important towns within the area

(b) Describe the major causes of desertification

(c) Explain two possible ways of combating this hazard.
explanation:
(b) Major causes of desertification include:

(i) Shifting of climatic zones e.g natural shifting or encroachment of the Sahara Desert towards the North estimated to be 50km per year

(ii) Climatic variations - very low rainfall

(iii) Human activities such as deforestation and overgrazing

(c) Two possible ways of combating desert encroachment include:

(i) Large scale irrigation system - This will enable water to be available in the soil and consequently promote the growth of vegetation in the area.

(ii) Afforestation - This involves the planting of trees which act as barriers to the speed of winds carrying sand from the desert

(b) Account for the location of each of the industries named in (a) above.
explanation:
(a) One cottage industry and one manufacturing industry in Western Industrial Zone are Textile and Cement industries respectively

(b) Reasons for the Location of Textile Industries:

Reasons for location of cement industries:

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Explain any four factors which limit the rate of development in the southern coastlands of Nigeria

(b) In what four ways can the development of the region be enhanced?
explanation:
(a) Four factors which limit the rate of development in the Southern Coastlands of Nigeria are:

(i) Swampy terrain of the area. The coastland is swampy due to high rainfall and nearness to the coast hence poor development of the area

(ii) Problem of erosion which has resulted in severe land loss in the area. This also makes fishing difficult.

(iii) The invasion of canals, creeks and lagoons by water hyacinth seriously limits the rate of development of the area as it makes transportation and fishing very difficult.

(iv) Exploration and exploitation of crude oil resulting in oil spillage, this also affects the rate of development of the area.

(b) Four ways in which the development of the region can be enhanced include:

(i) The establishment of River Basin Development Authorities to bring some infrastructural facilities to the region

(ii) The establishment of the Nigerian Institute for Oceanography and Marine Research is useful to collect data to ensure large fishing concerns to the area in order to increase its infrastructural development and economic prospect.

(iii) Construction of modern highways or flyovers to link the port towns and cities to reduce inaccessibility

(iv) Petroleum prospecting companies should allocate a reasonable percentage of their earnings to the development of the area.

subject: Geography
q:
Give an account of irrigation agriculture in the Nile Basin under the following headings

(a) need for irrigation (b) areas (c) methods (d) crops growth (e) importance
explanation:
(a) The need for irrigation agriculture in the Nile Basin include

(i) To supply water to the soil

(ii) To fight drought

(iii) To improve the environment where plants grow

(b) Areas of irrigation agriculture include:

(i) Areas around the Nile Valley especially in Egypt and Sudan which have a desert type of climate.

(c) Methods of irrigation include;

(i) Basin or flooding method - when water overflows the Nile banks and water the areas around the place

(ii) Perennial method -Involves the damming of the river to create artificial reservoir where water can be channeled to farms

(iii) The Shaduf method -Involves the collection of water from river channels using buckets or water bags and pouring it on the farms.

(d) Crops grown include rice, cotton, maize, sugarcane, vegetables, millet, ground-nut, etc.

(e) Importance of irrigation agriculture

(i) It ensures the cultivation of 2-3 crops in a year

(ii) No need to rely on rainfall

(iii) Water is made available to crops all the year round

(iv) It ensures bumber harvest from crops.

subject: Geography
q:
Write explanatory notes on lumbering in Zaire under the following headings

(a) areas (b) factors (c) importance (d) problems (e) possible solutions
explanation:
(a) Areas of lumbering in Zaire include the forest regions of Huri and Equateur provinces

(b) Factors of lumbering include:

(i) The dense equatorial forest is a source of valuable timber

(ii) River Zaire and its tributaries serve as means of transporting the logs to the sawmills or ports for export

(iii) Ready market for the sawn timber both locally and externally

(iv) There is high demand for timber as fuel

(c) Importance of lumbering include:

(i) Okoume (soft wood) is used for making plywood while Limba and Mahogany are used for construction work and furniture making

(ii) It provides employment to many people

(iii) Timber provides foreign exchange for the country

(iv) Timber provides raw materials for plywood industries

(v) Hardwoods are used for boat building because of their durability.

(d) Problems of Lumbering in Zaire include:

(i) High cost of surveying economic trees because there are many species around

(ii) Felling of trees is difficult and expensive because the trees are close together

(iii) Many of the trees have buttresses which make felling difficult

(iv) Presence of rapids and waterfalls makes transportation of logs difficult

(v) High rate of deforestation .without corresponding afforestation.

(e) Possible solution to the above problems

(i) Only valuable tree should be grown

(ii) Taungya system of farming whereby farmers grow crops and later plant economic tre -as should be adopted

(iii) Two economic seedlings sh )uld be grown where one has been cut

(iv) Roads should be constructed in some areas as to ensure easy transportation of logs.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Give an account of the economic importance or rocks

(b) Describe the characteristics and mode of formation of igneous rocks
explanation:
(a) Economic importance of rocks:

(i) The appearance and characteristic features of the land are influenced by the type of underlying rock

(ii) Rocks give rise to either highlands or lowlands e.g basement complex: rock give rise to highlands, while sedimentary rocks gi ie rise to lowlands

(iii) Most of the rock types are rich sources of minerals e.g petroleum is formed from sedimentary rock while gold is from igneous rock

(iv) Rocks Gan be quarried to form gravel used in house and road construction

(b) Characteristics of Igneous rock are:

(i) They exist in crystals i.e they are crystaline in structure

(ii) They do not occur in layer or strata

(iii) They do not contain fossils Mode of formation of Igneous rock: Igneous rock is formed from the cooling and solidification of molten magma ejected from below the earth crust. If the molten magma cools before it reaches the surface of the soil, it is called plutonic or intrusive igneous rock, e.g granite. But if it cools on the surface of the earth, it is called extrusive or volcanic igneous rock, e.g Basalt rock.

subject: Geography
q:
With the aid of well-labelled diagrams describe three main differences between the landform of any two of the following pairs

(a) Zeugens and yardangs

(b) Waterfalls and gorges

(c) Ria coasts and fiord coasts

(d) Levees and deltas.
explanation:
(a) Three main differences between Zeugen and Yardangs:

(i) in Zeugen, the hard and soft layer of rocks are arranged in horizontal bands while those ef yardangs are arranged in vertical bands.

(ii) In Zeugen there must be lines of fissure in order for wind abras to perform its work but in yardangs, there will be re. lines of fissure

(iii) Yardangs are easy to form than Zeugen. 

(b) Differences between fiord coast and ria coast:

(i) Fiord coasts have been glaciated, rias have not

(ii) The sides of fiord are higher and steeper than those of ria

(iii) The ria gets deeper at its seaward end whereas the deepest part of a fiord is usually some distance up the fiord

(iv) Fiords are deeper than ria coasts.

subject: Geography
q:
Describe either the Tropical Monsoon or the Cool Temperate Western Margin (British type) climatic region under the following headings
(a) Climate
(b) Vegetation
(c) Human activities
explanation:
(a) Climate of cool temperate western margin (British type): The mean annual temperature is 13°C. The warmest month is 17°C while the coldest month is 4°C. Climate is very good for mental alertness and maximum comfort. Rainfall is between 600-800mm.

(b) Vegetation: Presence of deciduous forest - trees shed their leaves in the cold season to protect themselves against the winter snow and frost. Tempe-rate hardwood include oak, elm, ash, birch, beech horn bean, eucalyptus and horse chestnut.

(c) Human Activities, the main human activities are;

(i) Marketing and gardening Vegetable farms located in city centres where crops like cucumber, beet root, cauliflower, lettuce are cultivated

(ii) Mixed farming: Wheat barley are the main arable crops grown while cattle especially dairy, beef, cattle, sheep, pigs are reared

subject: Geography
q:
Choose any one of the following vegetation types:

(a) Cool temperature eastern margin (Laurentian type)

(b) Temperate grassland

(c) Tropical rainforest

Describe the vegetation type chosen under the following headings
(i) location
(ii) vegetation characteristics
(iii) economic importance
explanation:
(a) (i) Location of Tropical Rain Forest: Tropical forest is located between latitudes 5° and 1 C'° k:orth and South of the equator. The vegetation is found in Amazon basin of South America, the Coast of West Africa, Zaire, Malaysia, Indonesia and Columbia.

(b) Characteristics of Rain Forest are:

(i) The forest is green throughout the year

(ii) It contains very many families and species of trees including lianas or climbers and epiphytes

(iii) There are three distinct lavers of trees, i.e the top layer, middle layer and lower layers.

(iv) Economic trees Uke Iroko, Obeche, Mahogany, African Walnut, etc are found

(v) There are undergrowth at the base of the forest. 

(c) Economic Importance of the Rain Forest:

(i) Durable and valuable hardwoods are got from the region, e.g Ebony, Iroko, Obeche, Mahogany, etc

(ii) Cultivation of cash crops like oil palm, cocoa, rubber, etc

(iii) very little small scale livestock farming is practised.

subject: Geography
q: Discuss any four ways in which man has interfered with his natural environment.
explanation:
Four ways in which man has interfered with his natural environment are:

(i) Human intervention is caused by the increasing pressures with the rise in human populations, activities and demand for basic necessities of life such as housing. Such activities have caused modifications of the climate, landforms, soil, vegetation and drainage network in many parts of the world

(ii) Those human activities that are related to agriculture, grazing, mining, forest exploitation, water resources management, etc, have some damaging effect on the environment

(iii) Some of the well-known environmental hazards such as soil and coastal erosion, flooding, deforestation, land subsistence, atmospheric and water pollution are known to affect the environment.

(iv) Some aspects of human intervention have also helped to sustain the natural environment, e.g afforestation, land reclamation, artificial fertilization and irrigation schemes.

subject: Geography
q: Account for the high population growth rates in the developing countries of the world
explanation:
Reasons for the high population growth rates in the developing countries of the world include the following:

(i) Polygamous Marriage: African men believe in marrying many wives in order to give birth to many children so as to give or produce cheap labour for farming.

(ii) Child bearing was regarded as source of wealth and higher social class among members of the community.

(iii) Improvement in agriculture leading to plentiful and varied food supply

(iv) Reduction in inter-tribal conflicts and wars

(v) Knowledge of the causes of infectious diseases and how to prevent them

(vi) Better sanitation

(vii) Reduced infant mortality rate as a result of better health care

(viii) Early marriage among the vast majority of the people

(ix) Improved health services

(x) Modernisation and industrialisation make standard of living to improve.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Identify the respective functions of urban and rural settlements

(b) Discuss three areas of interdependence between rural and urban settlement.
explanation:
(a)(i) The functions of rural settlements include:

(i) Agricultural activities as the main occupation of the people, i.e growing of food and cash crops

(ii) Pastoral farming as an occupation provided there is plenty of grasses for the livestock

(iii) Fishing function provided there are rivers

(iv) Small scale shopping

(v) Lumbering function provided there are forest trees

(ii) Functions of urban settlements:

(i) Industrial (production) function - presence of industries 

(ii) Commercial function which involves heavy trading

(iii) Administrative functions as urban areas are usually the sites of administration

(iv) Socio-cultural function as urban areas are places where schools, universities, foreign embassy offices are found

(v) Other primary occupations like mining or fishing can also be engaged in urban centres

(vi) Residential function as majority of people live in urban areas.

(b) Three areas of interdependence between rural and urban settlements are

(i) Rural settlements are sources of food for the urban settlements

(ii) Urban settlements are usually the sources of manufactured goods or food for the rural settlements

(iii) Urban settlements may also serve as administrative headquarters of the rural settlements.

subject: Geography
q: With specific examples, describe the role of road transportation in the promotion of national integration in any country of your choice
explanation:
The role of road transportation in the promotion of national integration in Ghana include the following:

(ii) Road transportation makes goods which are produced elsewhere available to other parts of the country

(ii) Transportation makes for regional specialisation in production. If goods are not moved, each region will produce less

(iii) Road development helps to reduce waste from over production e.g transportation of food from rural areas to urban centres

(iv) Construction of roads can also help in the development of large towns and cities

(v) It helps in national defense as troops can easily be moved by road to places where they are needed

(vi) it strengthens national unity as people from different parts of Ghana easily interact with one another.

subject: Geography
q: With the aid of specific examples, discuss the factors that limit industrial development in Tropical Africa.
explanation:
Factors that limit industrial development in Tropical Africa are:

(i) Inadequate Capital: Most of the entrepreneur are poor with low levels of savings. Banks are not wiling to give out loans because they do not have collateral security

(ii) Inadequate skilled manpower: Majority of work force are unskilled because of their low level of education and training So skilled labour is scarce

(iii) Small Market: Most industries in Africa have small market because of the small size of population and low purchasing power

(iv) Inadequate Infrastructure: Good roads, electricity, medical facilities are scarce hence low industrial development

(v) Shortage of Raw Materials: Raw materials are not locally available hence the industries have to depend on imported raw materials

(vi) Unfavourable industrial and administrative policies: Government at times makes some policies which are detrimental to the growth of some industries, e.g ban on the importation of wheat almost grounded all the flour mill plants in the country.

q:

(b) Choose any one of these countries and name:
(i) three major products which Nigeria exports to her
(ii) three major products which Nigeria imports from her

(c) Discuss two factors responsible for the present pattern of trade between Nigeria and that country

(d) Describe two effects of this observed pattern of trade on the economy of Nigeria
explanation:
(a) Three major countries outside Africa that trade with Nigeria are:
(i) Britain
(ii) United States of America and
(iii) Japan

(b) Three major products which Nigeria exports to Britain are:
(i) Petroleum
(ii) Cocoa
(iii) Palm oil.

Three major products which Nigeria imports from Britain are
(i) Motor vehicles
(ii) Aircrafts and
(iii) Ships.

(c) Two factors responsible for the present pattern of trade between Nigeria and Britain include

(i) Uneven distribution of natural resources: Natural resources are unevenly distributed hence there is variation in the type of goods produced in different countries and this is why these goods have to be exchanged

(ii) Differences in technical know-how and expertise: There is a big difference in the technical know-how between Nigeria and Britain resulting in the production of certain goods. This results in the exchange of goods between the two countries

(d) Two effects of this observed pattern of trade on the economy of Nigeria are:

(i) It enables goods not produced in the countries to be available

(ii) It enables the country to get foreign exchange:

(iii) the Jos Plateau, the Udi Hills, the Western Highlands and the Obudu plateau
(iv) one lowland region in the north and one in the south

(b) Describe the importance of the River Niger to the economy of Nigeria.
explanation:
(b) Importance of River Niger to the economy are:

(i) G- aeration of Hydro-Electric Power (HEP) i.e electricity

(ii) It also serves as a means of transportation

(iii) It provides water for irrigation of farmlands

(iv) It provides employment to some people

(v) Fishing is also carried out in the river

(v) It also provides water for domestic and industrial purposes.

subject: Geography
q: With relevant sketch maps, discuss the relationship between climate and vegetation in Nigeria.
explanation: There is a positive relationship between vegetation and climate particularly rainfall and temperature. The intensity of rainfall is high in the extreme south giving rise to mangrove swamp forests. The rainfall decreases towards the north till when the rainfall is low in extreme north, e.g Borno area. The decrease in rainfall also accounts for the decrease in the thickness of the vegetaton from rain forest to grassland. In other words, the heavier the rainfall the thicker the vegetation will be. Hence south with heavy rainfall results in rainforest while the north with little rainfall results in grassland or savanna.

subject: Geography
q:
Give an account of the factors that have aided the location of any three of the following

(a) Textile mills in Kaduna

(b) Oil refinery in Warri

(c) Pulp and paper mill in Oku Iboku

(d) Cement factory in Ewekoro
explanation:
(a) Factors which favour the location of textile mills in Kaduna are:

(i) Availability of raw material. The raw material which is cotton is massively grown in Zaira and Kaduna

(ii) Presence of large market - Clothing materials produced from the mills are easily disposed off because of big market

(iii) Availability of labour. There is abundance of labour for the processing of cotton to its products.

(iv)Availability of power - Power, e.g electricity is readily available for normal operation of the plant. 

(5) Factor which favour the location of oil refinery in Warn are:

(i) Availability of raw material which is crude oil

(ii) Nearness to market as products produced from the refinery are used in the south

(iii) reduction in transportation cost. Cost of transporting crude oil is reduced as crude oil is processed within the area where the oil is found

(iv) Nearness to seaports from where the products can easily be exported.

(c) Factors which favour the location of cement factory in Ewekoro are:

(i) Nearness to raw materials which is limestone

(ii) Reduction in the cost of transporting the raw materials

(iii) Availability of labour

(iv) Availability of power.

subject: Geography
q:
Discuss the characteristics of either the Western Highlands or the North Central Highlands of Nigeria under tile following headings;
(a) Relief
(b) Drainage
(c) Soils
(d) Vegetation
(e) Human activities
explanation:
(a) Relief of Western Highland: The area lies between 300 and 600m above the sea level. The hills'found include Idanre (the highest of 900m). Effon Alaye, Epemi, Akpata Hills, etc

(b) Drainage: The area of highland is drained by three rivers which flow directly into the Alantic Ocean. These rivers are Ogun, Osun and Osse

(c) Soils: The 'easement complex rocks give rise to ferruginous soils which are acidic but fertile. They have high clay content and good drainage

(d) Vegetation: The area is mainly rainforest in the southern part with luxuriant evergreen forest trees like I roko, Obeche, Mahogany, etc, while the Northern area is covered by derived Savanna with tall grasses and short scanty trees,

(e) Human activities: The main occupation of the people is farming especially crops like cocoa, kola, rubber, cotton and tobacco. Other are yam, cassava, etc. They are also involved in mining like limestone in Ewekoro, Ukpila, Shagamu. Other minerals include marble and gold. Gold is mined in Ilesha.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Discuss the problems of transportation on the
(i) Niger-Benue waterway
(ii) Lagos-Kano railway line

(b) Suggest possible solutions to these problems
explanation:
(a) Problems of transportation on:

(i) Niger- Benue waterway include:

(1) Change in the volume of water with season. There is increase in volume of water during the rainy season which favours easy navigation but it is a problem during the dry season.

(2) Presence of rapids and cataracts - These are obstacles which inhibit easy transportation in the rivers

(3) Presence of debris. Niger-Benue waterway are at times filled with debris of forest leaves, broken branches of trees. These debris inhibit easy navigation in Niger-Benue waterway

(ii) The problems of transportation on Lagos-Kano railway line include:

(1) Long distance between the place involved. The distance between Lagos -Kano is too long and boring

(2) Problems with the rail tracks. Most of the rail tracks (Gauges) are worn out and outdated

(3) Presence of only one lane. Despite the importance of the route, it has only one lane as this poses a problem of allowing one train to wait till the other has passed Solution: As for the Niger-Benue waterway effort should be made to remove these cateracts and rapids in some areas so that navigation will be smooth. Solution: As for the Lagos-Kano railway line effort should be made to construct another lane so that one train doesn't wait for the other to pass.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Describe the methods of irrigation agriculture in either the Nile Valley or the Niger Valley

(b) Give reasons for the irrigation agriculture in any one of the river valleys

(c) Discuss the problems associated with irrigation agriculture in the river valley you have chosen above
explanation:
(a) The methods of irrigation agriculture in the Nile valley are:

(i) Basin or flooding mathod - involves the seasonal flooding of the nearby lands in the Nile Valley during which the flood water is trapped and cannot flow back into the Nile Valley

(ii) Perennial Method - involves the damming of rivers to create artificial reservoirs which can supply water to irrigation canals at any time of the year

(iii) The Shaduf Method - The oldest method during which farmers collect water from river channel, either with a bucket or a water bag, and pour it on their farms.

(b) Reasons for irrigation agriculture in Nile valley are:

(i) To supply water to the soil as little or no rainfall is experienced around these areas

(ii) To fight drought as Egypt experiences desert climate

(iii) To produce crops all year round.

(c) Problems associated with irrigation agriculture in the Nile Valley are:

(i) There is the problem of fluctuation in the level of water used for irrigation purposes

(ii) Presence of excess of mineral salt like sodium which are injurious to plant growth

(iii) Over-flooding could be disastrous

(iv) Construction of many dams disturbs normal flow of water.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Discuss the main features of rotational bush fallowing in West Africa

(b) Describe the major factors affecting the practice of bush fallowing in West Africa

(c) Explain the problems associated with this farming method.
explanation:
(a) The main features of rotational bush fallowing in West Africa are:

(i) There is abundant land

(ii) It is practised where the population is low

(iii) Farmers settlement is fixed as he only moves from one farmland to another

(iv) Only annual crops are grown

(v) Simple farm tools are used

(b) Factors affecting the practice of bush fallowing in West Africa

(i) Lack of sufficient land

(ii) Increase in population

(iii) Poor fertility of the soil

(c) Problems associated with bush fallowing:

(i) Destruction of valuable forest through land clearing and bush burning

(ii) It leads to fragmentation of land if there is increase in population

(iii) It allows for cultivation of only seasonal or annual crops like maize, yam, cassava, etc

(iv) The system can lead to soil erosion

(v) A lot of money, time and energy is spent on preparing the land for very little agricultural returns.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Draw a sketch map of Africa and on it mark and name the following dams:
(i) Kariba
(ii) Aswan
(iii) Cabora Bassa
(iv) Akosombo
(v) Owen

(b) With reference to specific examples in Africa, discuss the importance of hydro-electric power projects to the economy

(c) Explain the problems facing the development of hydro-electric power projects in Tropical Africa.
explanation:
(b) Importance of Hydro-Electric Power Projects to the Economy of Africa:

(i) Provision of power i.e electricity, e.g Akosombo dam

(ii) Hydro-electric project like Aswan dam also provides water for irrigation of farmland.

(iii) Dams are also used to control floods

(iv) Fishing is done nearly in all dams in Africa for tourism and recreation

(v) It generates revenue as it serves as centre for tourism and recreation

(vi) It is also used as good water communication.

(c) Problems of H.E.P development in Tropical Africa are:

(i) Water shortages may disrupt the output of hydro electric units

(ii) Mechanical failures could also be a problem

(iii) Initial cost of construction is usually very high

(iv) There is high cost of resettlement of displaced people

(v) Lack of effective demand for the power generated.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Describe the methods of lumbering used in either Zaire or Nigeria

(b) Discuss the problems associated with the exploitation of timber in the area

(c) Using relevant examples, discuss the economic importance of lumbering in Equatorial Africa.
explanation:
(a) Methods of Lumbering Used in Zaire are:

(i) The lumbermen search the forest to identify the particular tree species wanted

(ii) The trees are felled mainly by axe or hand saw or power saw

(iii) To cut a buttress tree a platform of 3-4 m above the ground is made on which the lumbermen stand to fell the tree

(iv) When the tree is felled, its branches are cut off and the trunks are carried by tractors to the nearest river, road or railway for onward movement to the sawmill of port for export.

(b) Problems Associated with the Exploitation of Timber in the Area:

(i) Heterogeneous nature of trees. Costs high amount of money to identify good ones

(ii) Difficulties in felling the trees because the trees are very close together

(iii) Trees with buttress roots are more difficult to fell

(iv) Many valuable species of trees are not felled because they are not known by the overseas consumers

(v) Rapids and fall! on the river make transportation difficult

(vi) Problem o deforestation without replacement. 

(c) Economic Importance of Lumbering in Equatorial Africa Include:

(i) The timber is_exported—as sawn logs for foreign exchange

(ii) It is used for the manufacture of plywoods and veneer

(iii) It is used for building boats, making furniture, and in the construction of houses and bridges

(iv) Wood is used as fuel. e.g for drying tobacco guinea savanna and smoking of fish in riverine areas

(v) It provides raw materials for industries, e.g paper, toothpick and matches industries

(vi) It provides employment to many people.

subject: Geography
q:
Write a geographical account of ECOWAS under the following headings

(a) Aims and objectives

(b) Membership

(c) Possible solutions
explanation:
(a) Aims and Objectives of ECOWAS include:

(i) To increase trade between the member countries

(ii) To cooperate in cultural areas such as education, music and art

(iii) To promote cooperation and development in other economic activities such as industry, transport, energy, etc

(iv) To contribute to a common fund which could be used to compensate countries that may lose economically as a result if the organisation and development of foreign investment

(v) To establish a common currency eventually.

(b) Membership of ECOWAS: It is made up of sixteen West African countries which are Nigeria, Benin Republic, Togo, Ghana, Cote d'Ivoire, Liberia, Sierra Loene, Guinea, Guinea Bissau, Gambia, Senegal, Mauritania, Mali, Burkina Faso and Niger.

(c) Possible solutions to these problems:

(i) Payment of annual dues should be compulsory as any member who refuses to pay should be suspended

(ii) Proper orientation among the members as there should be equality in the discussion and formulation of resolutions

(iii) A neutral body should be set up to execute programmes or projects for the organisation

(iv) President of ECOWAS should be rotated among the Heads of State of each country

(v) The formation of a common currency, should be implemented to facilitate inter-regional trade.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) State three reasons to show that the earth is spherical

(b) Describe three effects of the revolution of the earth

(c) Calculate the noon altitude of the sun at the Tropic of Capricorn on June 21
explanation:
(a) Three reasons to show that the earth is spherical include

(i) The circumnavigation of the earth

(ii) Sunrise and sunset

(iii) Eclipse of the moon.

(b) Three effects of the revolution of the earth include the follow

(i) It varies the length of day and night.

(ii) It causes seasons

(iii) It changes the altitude of the midday sun at differ times of the year

(i) Varying the length of day and night and seasons:- The earth is inclined at an angle of 66½ °N. In the Northern hemisphere in winter, during the month of December as we go northwards , hours and darkness increase. At artic circle (66½ °N) the sun never rises and there is darkness for the whole day around December 22nd. In summer around June, the conditions are reversed. Daylight increases as we go poleward. At the artic circle the sun never set at mid-summer (June 21st) and there is a complete 24 hour period of continuous daylight in the Southern hemisphere: the same process takes place except that the conditions are reversed. When it is summer in the Northern hemisphere the Southern hemisphere will experience winter.

Changes in the altitude of the midday sun: The sun is overhead vertically at the equator for two days each year. The days are March 21st and September 23rd. These two days are called Equinoxes (meaning equal days any nights). Because of these two days, all parts of the world will experience equal days and equal nights. After the March equinox, the sun appears to move northward and vertically overhead at the Tropic of Cancer (23½°N) around June 21st. This is known as June or summer solstice (when the Northern Hemisphere will have its longest day shortest nights). By December 22nd, the sun will be overhead at the tropic or capricorn (23½ºS) called December or winter solstice, i.e when the Southern Hemisphere has its longest day and shortest night

(c) To calculate the noon altitude of the sun at the Tropic of capricorn on June 21. The sun is overhead at the Tropic of capricorn i.e, 23½°S. Angle at right angle = 90° since the angle at the Tropic of capricorn is 23½°S. The noon altitude on June 21 of the sun is 90° - 23½ºS = 66½

subject: Geography
q:
(a) What are the similarities and differences between the lines of latitude and longitude?

(b) An international cricket match between The Gambia and India is scheduled to start in Banjul (longitude 16°W) at 9.00a.m local time on 26th June. Calculate the time and day that listeners will have to tune to their radios for the start of the commentary at (i) Bombay (74°E) (ii) Pittsburg (89°W).
explanation:
Similarities between the lines of latitude and longitude:

(i) Both latitude and longitude are used to locate position of places

(ii) Both are measured in degrees

(iii) Both are angular distance of a point on the earth's surf

Lines of Latitudes Lines of longitude

(I) Lines of latitude run from West to East between one place to another.
(ii) Lines of latitude are used to calculate distance between one place to another
(iii) Lines of latitude cannot be used to calculate local time.
(iv) The latitudinal extent of the globe is from 90°N and 90°S 

- Lines of longitude run from North to South between one place and another.
- Lines of longitude cannot be used to calculate distance between one place and another
- Lines of longitude are used in calculating local time.
- The longitudinal extent of the globe is from 180° E to 180°W 

(b)(i) Bombay (74°E)
(a) Find the longitude differences between Banjul and Bombay = (16°W and 74°E) = 74°E + 16°W = 90°
(b) Convert 90° to time. Note 15° = 1 hour
15°      6hrs
90° =   6 hours

(c) Since Bombay is east of Banjul, the 6 hours are added to the 9.00am. i.e 9.00am + 6 hours = 3.00p.m the day i.e 26th June. The listeners in Bombay will listen to commentary at 3.00pm on 26/6

(b) (ii) Pittsburg (80°W) 
(a) Find the longitude difference between Banjul and Pittsburg = (80°W - 16°W) = 64°
(b) Convert 65° to time. Note 15° = 1 hr
15       4 hrs
          64
          60
       4° x 4 mins = 16 minutes
Time difference = 4hrs 16 minutes

(c) Since Pittsburg is West of Banjul, the 4 hrs 16 mins are subtracted from 9.00a.m
i.e 9.00a.m - 4hrs 16 mins = 9.00
                                              4.16
                                              4.44 hours
The time and date listeners in Pittsburg will listen to the commentary is 4.44am the same day 26th June.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Describe the characteristics of any two of the following types of landforms
(i) delta
(ii) atoll
(iii) caldera

(b) Explain with the aid of diagrams the mode of formation of each of the landforms you have chosen
explanation:
(a)(i) The characteristics of a delta include:

(i) The branching of rivers into channels called distributaries

(ii) Large deposits of weathered materials

(iii) It is formed at the mouth of rivers

(iv) It takes up the shape of a fan

(b) The r of formation of a delta is influenced by the following factors; 

(i) Active vertical and lateral erosion in the upper course of the river to provide extensive sediments to be deposited as deltas

(ii) The coast should be sheltered or tic',

(iii) The sea adjoining the delta should be shallow or else the load will disappear in the deep waters

(iv), Absence of lakes in the river course to filter off the sediments

(v) Absence of strong current running at right angles to the mouth so as not to wash away sediments 

(b)(i) Characteristics of caldera:

(i) It is formed from volcanic activities

(ii) It is a depression on volcano cones with steep sides

(iii) Depression of magma gives rise to caldera

(iv) It may contain water called caldera lake. Caldera is formed when composite volcano explodes violently, its top is blown off and disintegrates into a mass of rocks and ashes, leaving the crater greatly enlarged. The huge crater-like depression is coiled caldera. It can also be formed through subsidence. The depression may result in accumulation of water to form caldera lake

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Name three types of mountains and give a good example of each

(b) Describe the process of formation of any type of mountains

(c) In what four ways are mountains of great importance to man?.
explanation:
(a) Three types of mountains are:

(i) Block mountain e.g the Hunstruck mt. of Germany

(ii) Fold mountain e.g the Himalayas

(iii) Volcanic mountain, e.g Fuji mountain of Japan

(b) Mode of formation of Fold mountains

(i) Folding is caused by large stale h Drizontal earth movement

(ii) Stresses, caused by expansion or contraction of some parts of the earth, are set up in the earth's crust

(iii) Such stresses subject the reeks to compressive forces

(iv) The compressive forces produce wrinkling or folding of the earth's crust

(v) The upfold of the wrinkles are called anticlines while the downfold are synclines.

(c) Four ways in which mountains are of great importance to man; 

(i) High  mountain can result in the formation of rainfall i.e relief rainfall

(ii) Mountain serves as the source or origin of all rivers

(iii) Mountains are rich sources of minerals Stones found in mountains can be quarried for building construction

subject: Geography
q:
(a)(i) Name two distinct types of soil erosion
(ii) Locate an area where each type is most common

(b) Outline any three factors that have been responsible for any one of the types of soil erosion named above

(c) Describe any three measures being taken to solve the problem of soil erosion in any one of the areas.
explanation:
(a)(i) Two types of soil erosion are;

(i) Water erosion and Wind erosion

(ii) The area where water erosion is most common is moist humid area of the tropic (i.e rainforest areas), while wind erosion is common in the arid and semi arid areas especially in the desert

(b) Three factors responsible for water erosion are

(i) Excessive rainfall

(ii) Impervious nature of underlying rock

(iii) Increase in declivity (slope). The greater the slope, the greater the velocity and degree of erosion.

(c) Three measures being taken to solve the problem of soil erosion in tropical area of the world are:

(i) Contour ploughing. This involves the making of ridges across the slope to reduce surface run off

(ii) Strip cropping: In slopy areas, strip cropping is done to also reduce the surface run off of the water

(iii) Proper channelisation. This involves the construction of gutters to channel away the large volume of water caused by erosion

Explain the factors responsible for the very high population density in the

(a) North-East of USA

(b) Indus and Ganges deltas.
explanation:
(a) Factors responsible for the very high population density in North - East of USA are:

(i) Presence of great deposits of iron and coal

(ii) Location of many industries

(iii) Favourable climate conducive for human habitation

(iv)Availability of jobs which make population to increase

(iv) Heavy trading of manufactured goods within and outside the industrial zone of USA.

(b) Factors which favour the high population density in the Indus and Ganges deltas

(i) Abundance of rainfall which ensures smooth agricultural activities

(ii) The soils are very fertile

(iii) Intensive agricultural activities attract people to the area (they grow 2-3 crops in a year)

(iv) Favourable climate conducive for intensive farming.

explanation:

Transportation by Road Transportation by rail

(1) Provides door-to-door service
(2) It can only cover short distances

- Does not provide door-to-door service

subject: Geography
q:
Explain any six factors each which have favoured the development of any two of the following industries:

(a) Iron and steel industry in the Ruhr region

(b) Ship building in Japan

(c) Chemical industry in eastern United States of America

(d) Textile industries in Great Britain
explanation:
(a) Six factors which have favoured the development of Iron and Steel industry in the Ruhr region

(i) Availability of coal which provides cheap source of coke for processing iron ore

(ii) Availability of Iron ore

(iii) Presence of Rhine which is one of the most navigable rivers in Europe, enables raw materials and goods to be moved between North Sea and Ruhr region

(iv) Good network of excellent roads linking Rhine valley with the rest of West Germany

(v) Abundance of soft, clear water

(vi) By-products of Iron and steel industry leads to the development of chemical industries

(b) Six factors which have favoured the development of Textile industries in Great Britain; 

(i) Availability of raw materials, e.g cotton, wool, silk and linen etc.

(ii) Presence of coal which makes it possible for steam power to replace water power

(iii) Abundance of soft water which is essential to removing the grease from wool before it is spun

(iv) Abundance of skilled labour

(v) Availability of large market

(vi) Well developed centres and ports for the export of textile.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) With the aid of appropriate diagrams, describe the distinguishing characteristics of any three types of urban settlements

(b) Discs any four problems of urbanization
explanation:
Three types of urban settlement include:
(i) Cities
(ii) Conurbation
(iii) Megalopolis

(i) City: A city is made up of large towns with great( number of people and services. In a city there are towns which combined together to function as one

(ii) Conurbation: Is made up of two or more towns join E together through growth but which may still retain thE identities. These two towns are separated from eac other but through expansion or growth have joined together. But despite this, they still retain their identities

(iii) Megalopolis: This is the largest of tl-urban settlements and includes the joining of man cities to form an extensive urban area. 

(b) Four problems of urbanization include:

(i) Inadequate transport facilities. Increase in the number of people in the urban area often leads to problem of transportation

(ii) Overuse and deterioration of available social services. Most of the social services available,. e.g electricity, stadium, school, hospital are overused and this leads to their deterioration

(iii) There is an aggravated problem of unemployment and underemployment in the cities as well as reduce labour force in the rural areas

(iv) There is the problem of undue increase in human and vehicular traffic.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) With specific examples give four reasons why countries exchange goods and services

(b) Account for the low volume of trade between Nigeria and the rest of Africa.
explanation:
(a) Four reasons why countries exchange goods and services

(i) Natural resources; Natural resources of the world are unevenly distributed e.g Nigeria's petroleum is sold to Brazil in exchange for vehicles, sugar and communication equipment.

(ii) Degree of Industrial Development; Due to high technical knowledge and expertise which a country has in the production of goods and electronic equipment than other countries e.g Japan specialises in the production of electronic equipment

(iii) Some crops are more suited to certain climatic condition. Crops like cocoa, cotton, etc, grow in tropical regions while crops like wheat, barley and oats grow well in temperate regions

(iv) Geographical Situation; Some countries have situations which enable them to take part in trade between contrasting economies e.g Singapore handles much of South East Asia's trade

(b) Reasons for low volume of trade between Nigeria and the rest of Africa are

(i) African countries produce goods which are very similar, leaving little or no room for exchange

(ii) Nigerians have a high taste for imported goods from foreign countries. These goods are not produced by other African countries

(iii) Nigeria's foreign exchange earnings from African countries is very low - about 20%

(iv) Prices of goods offered by African countries are usually very low compared with foreign countries

(v) African countries demand for very low quantity of goods unlike foreign countries.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) On an outline map of West Africa show and name:
(i) two countries important for the production of rubber
(ii) one important town in the rubber producing area in each country

(iv) one important town in the groundnut producing area in each country

(b) Describe the process of production of either rubber or groundnuts, in the country shown on your map

(c) Explain any three factors which hinder the large scale production of either rubber or groundnuts in WestAfrica.
explanation:
(b) Rubber is produced or extracted from the tre( a process called tapping. Early in the morning  rubber farmer goes to tap his rubber trees by making a cut on them. Below each cut he places a cup it which the milky juice caked latex drips. This is collected in pails in the evening. The latex is mixed with certain chemicals which make it to coagulate. it is rolled into sheets and smoked to a brown colt and are later sold to local buying agents.

(c) Three problems which may hinder the large scale production of rubber include:

(i) It takes the rubber trees long to mature - about 6 to 8 years

(ii) Presence of poor quality or low yielding varieties of rubber

(iii) Pests diseases attack on rubber also reduces the yield

subject: Geography
q:
Write a geographical account of fishing in Southern Africa under the following headings:

(a) important fishing areas and fishing ports

(b) Methods of fishing

(c) Major types of fish caught

(d) Preparation and marketing of fish

(e) Importance to the economy.
explanation:
(a) Important fishing areas and fishing ports in South Africa. Fishing is done in two ways

(i) Inland fishing- not very popular and it is only done in Rivers Orange and Great Fish River

(ii) Offshore fishing - Done along the entire coast of South Africa and also in deep seas i.e Atlantic and Indian oceans. Some of the fishing ports are port Elizabeth, Durban, East London.

(b) Methods of fishing. On a large scale the method of fishing involves-the use of  trawlers especially in the oceans

(c) Major type of fish caught. These include mackerel, herring, salmon, had etc.

(d) Preparation and marketing of fish. Fish caught are cleaned, refrigerated in cold stores and can be exp as raw fish. It can also be processed into usable canfish e.g sardines which are later consumed locally or exported 

(e) Fishing is very important for the following reasons:

(i) It provides rich source of protein for the people

(ii) Its exportation provides foreign exchange earning for the country

(iii) It also provides jobs for many people

(iv) It has resulted in the establishment of fish processing industries.

subject: Geography
q:
Discuss the distinguishing characteristics of each of any three of the following

(a) Plantation agriculture in East Africa

(b) Irrigation farming in the Nile Valley

(c) Pastoral nomadism in West Africa

(d) Fruits farming in Southern Africa.
explanation:
(a) Characteristics of plantation agriculture in East Africa

(i) Plantation agriculture requires a large area of land 

(ii) There should be large supply of cheap unskilled labour within the location of the plantation

(iii) Huge amok capital is required

(iv) Infrastructural facilities like good roads, electricity, pipe born water are required

(v) There be available skilled manpower

(vi) Modern farming techniques are used e.g use of chemicals, fertilizer etc.

(b) Pastoral nomadism in West Africa:

(i) It requires the movement of cattle with their herdsmen from one play another in search of pasture and water

(ii) It is more extensive than intensive method of cattle rearing

(iii) Thi herdsmen are illiterate as they do not know how to treat diseased animals

(iv) The cattle are subjected to love of feeding

(v) There is the constant clashes between cattle rearers and arable farmers.

(c) Fruit farming in South Africa

(i) Fruit farming is made possible as a result of favourable climate and this includes bright and sunny weather, rainfall of 500- 700mm and temperature of 28°C

(ii) Availability of fertile valleys where animal soil ex

(iii) Funds must be made available to boost production

(iv) Irrigation system is necessary for fruit farming

(v) the fruits produced have access to foreign markets.

subject: Geography
q:
Write a geographical account of petroleum mining in North Africa under the following headings:

(a) Major areas (b) Methods of mining (c) Transportation (d) Importance to the economy
explanation:
(a) Major areas of petroleum mining in North Africa include Libya, Algeria, Egypt and Tunisia

(b) Methods of r petroleum involves drilling

(i) The underground rocks are sampled and tested for traces of oil

(ii) When oil is disco a well is sunk to reach it by the help of a drilling rig or derrick

(iii) The oil is then pumped out, but where the enough pressure underground, the oil flows out on its own

(iv) A system of values called "Christmas tree" is ei to control the flow into tanks

(c) Transportation: Oil is usually transported through pipelines to the refineries tankers at the oil terminals can also be used in transportation of crude oil.

(d) Importance to the economy:

(i) provides foreign exchange earnings to the economy

(ii) It is refined to obtain petrol, gas, kerosene, etc

(iii) B products are used in making insecticides, petroleum jelly, lubricating oil etc

(iv) Petroleum provides raw mater associated industries

(v) It provides gainful employment to some people
