subject: Geography
q:
(a) Distinguish between rotation and revolution of the earth

(b) Highlight any two effects of either rotation or revolution of the earth

(c) With the aid of a suitable diagram describe an eclipse of the sun
explanation:
(a) The difference between rotation and revolution of the earth. Rotation is the type of earth's movement in which it moves on its axis from west to east once in 24hrs making one day. Revolution is the second type of movement of the earth in which it revolves around th sun in 3651/4 days making a year.

(b) Two effects of rotation are the; 

(i) day and night

(ii) the deflection c winds and ocean currents

(c) The eclipse of the sun is formed when the moon comes between the sun and the earth. When there is an eclipse of the moon, shadow of tne moon is cast, on the earth which could result in either partial or full darkness on the earth. The moon prevents the sun rays from getting to the earth. The positions of the sun, moon and the earth in an eclipse of the sun are shown in the diagram below

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Describe the characteristics of each of the following rock types
(i) Granite
(ii) Limestone
(iii) Graphite

(b) Explain how each rock type is formed
explanation:
(a) The characteristics of the following are; 
(i) Granite

(i) Granite contains large crystals

(ii) They do not occur in layers or strata

(iii) They do not contain fossils which are the remains of plants and animals embedded in rocks.

Limestone:

(i) Limestone is arranged in layers or stata

(ii) They contain fossils

(iii) The rock is non-crystaline in structure

(iii)Graphite:

(i) Graphite is not crystaline

(ii) It is arranged in layers or strata

(iii) It may contain fossils

(b)(i) Granite is formed from the cooling and solidification of molten magma ejected from below the earth crust.  It cools before reaching the surface of the earth hence granite is called a plutonic or intrusive igneous rock

(ii) Limestone is formed from the remains of living organisms such as corals or shellfish. These remains have been cemented together under the influence of water to form organic sedimentary rock

(iii) Graphite is formed from pre-existing sedimentary rock called clay which have changed or metamorphosed to a new rock as a result of the influence of heat and pressure. Graphite is an example of metamorphic rock.

subject: Geography
q:
Write explanatory notes on each of the following

(a) Land and sea breezes

(b) Temperature inversion

(c) Convectional rainfall
explanation:
(a) Land And Sca Freezes: Breeze exchange between the land and the sea is attributed to differential heating of land and sea. These local wind are common in areas very close to the sea. During the day, the land is heated much faster than the sea and it is cooler than the sea at night. As the land is heated during the day, it causes the air to rise, creating a low pressure situation. Air therefore blows from the sea to replace the heated and rising air on the land thereby resulting in lower temperature and relatively cool air on the land. This is sea breeze which blows from sea to land. At night, the land becomes cooler that-. the sea having lost much of its temperature at a much faster rate than the sea. Thus, there is a high pressure situation on the land and a relatively low pressure situation on the sea. Hence a land breeze blows from the land to the sea.

(b) Temperati.re inversion: This is the opposite of lapse rate and it means a rise in temperature with increasing altitude. It is not in all cases that temperature decreases with altitude. Temperature could be lower at the sea-level especially in valleys almost enclosed by mountains than at heights above such valley E.g temperature inversion is found in European Alps where cold and heavy al: collects in valleys which are almost totally enclosed by mountains

(c) Convectional rainfall: This is the main type of rainfall in the humid tropics. The heat generated by the sun is aL;le to make the surroundings including the sea and ocean to be hot. Because the air is hot, it is able to absorb very large amount of moisture. The warm and moist air is forced to rise rapidly by convection current. When the current rises very high and reaches the cold layers of the atmosphere which make it conducive for the moisture contained in tha currents to condense, rainfall immediately fo: ows the condensation of the moisture. This type of rain formed by this process is called convectional rainfall.

subject: Geography
q: Discuss the important features of Koppen's climatic classification
explanation:
The important features of Koppen; climatic classification is based on vegetation. It attempts to explain the variation of earth's climate by studying the connection between vegetation and climate. Koppean's classification of climates consists of five major climatic groups which are

A- This is moist and hot climate e.g tropical rainy climates with avenge mz.nthly temperature of above 18°C. This climate has no winter seasons. Annual rainfall is large and exceeds annual evaporation.

B - This is dry climate in which potential evaporation exceeds precipitation. Since there is no water surplus, no permanent streams originate in B climatic zones.

C - This refers to moist and warm climate e.g warm temperate climate, whose coldest month has an average temperatures of under 18°C but has above 3°C in at least one month. It has both summer  and winter seasons.

D - This is snowy and cold climate (cold - snow forest climate). The average temperature of its coldest month is under 3°C. The average temperature of the warmest month is above 10°C

E -This is polar icy climate (snow climate). The average temperature of its warmest month is below 10°C. This climate has no true summer. In order to differentiate between sub-divisions within these major groups, secondary letters are assigned to furnish more complete explanation e.g

F - moist all year
M -monsoon conditions
S - dry summer wet winter
V - dry winter, wet summer.

A third letter was added to include information about temperature e.g

a - long hot su. rimer
b - short, warm summer
c - short and moderate or cool summer
d - very cold am; dry winter
h - hot and dry most of the year
k - cold and dry most of the year.

This is how these letters are assigned to climatic belts e.g polar and sub-polar (sub arctic) regions begin with the letter E, to which are added either T (for tundra vegetation and conditions). The dry climates are assigned the letter W and S to differentiate between desert (BW) and steppe (BS) climate and vegetation conditions. BWK would refer to cool desert climate while DFe would refer to cold snowy forest climate with cool short summer.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Describe the characteristics of the tropical grassland vegetation

(b) Outline the economic values of this vegetation type to the inhabitants of the region
explanation:
(a) The characteristics of the tropical grass land vegetation include:

(i) Dry season lasts for 6-7 months

(ii) Tall trees reaching 2m dominate the vegetation

(iii) Scattered trees mainly in the river valley called savanna parkland

(iv) Around the equator the trees are bigger and more common than grasses called savanna woodland

(v) Towards the desert with less rainfall there are few trees with short grasses called savanna scrub. Some of the trees here e.g acacia, baobab, etc are deciducus (i.e shod the it leaves during the dry season)

(b) The economic values of this vegetation type to the inhabitants of the region include:

(i) Livestock farming (e.g cattle rearing) is the most important occupation E.g Hausa - Fulani cattle rearers, Massai of Kenya etc. are known to be heavily involved in livestock farming.

(ii) Grain cultivation: Some elements 3f grains such as millets, sorghum and maize are widely cultivated. Groundnut and cotton can also be cultivated

(ii:) Game reserves. Most of these grasslands serve as game reserves e.g National Park of South Africa and Yankari Same Reserves are centres of attraction for tourism.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Name two main types of environmental pollution and describe three causes of any one of them

(b) State three effects of pollution on man
explanation:
(a) Two main types of environmental pollution are:

(i) Air pollution
(ii) water pollution 

Three causes of water pollution include:

(I) The release of disease-producing (pathogenic) bacteria and viruses (biological pollution diseases

(ii) Undesirable ions and compounds in solution (chemical pollution) into water thereby changing its colour, taste and/or smell

(iii) Oil spillage - the bursting of oil pipes results in oil spillage into water thereby changing the colour, taste and smell of the water.

(b) Three effects of pollution on man are:

(i) Atmospheric pollution, which may be caused by fog reduces visibility. This poses a serious danger to motorists and pilots

(ii) Pollutants may have adverse psychological effects on man and animals e.g discomfort, chronic diseases, such as lung cancer and asthma, impairment of growth and death

(iii) Some pollutants are toxic and can kill plants e.g sulfur dioxide and hydrogen fluoride are toxic to plants from which man gets his food.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Explain two physical and two human factors which may favour a rapid growth of population

(b) Outline five problems that may arise from the rapid growth of population
explanation:
(a) Two physical factors which may attract or favour a rapid growth of population are:

(i) Favourable climate: This leads to dense population, e.g temperate regions like U.K. U.S.A Japan tend to have increased population because of good climate

(ii) Fertile soils. Fertile soils attract population, e.g fertile alluvial soil in the deltas of South East Asia, River Nile in Egypt have attracted dense population.

Two human factors which may favour a rapid growth of population are:

(i) Commercial activities, Commercial activities like heavy trading centres, and presence of ports are closely associated with population concentration

(ii) Mining activities. Mining activities in areas where minerals are available tend to attract high concentration of people.

(b) Five problems that may arise from rapid growth of population include:

(i) Accommodation problem

(ii) Transportation problem

(iii) High pressure on existing social amenities

(iv) High level of crimes

(v) Traffic congestion

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Identify three types of rural settlements and describe the main characteristics of each

(b) In what four ways are rural and urban settlements interdependent?
explanation:
(a) Three types of rural settlements are:

(i) Isolated settlement - One or a few houses only are found in a remote area

(ii) Two or more houses without shops, schools, or services also in remote area

(iii) Village - Many houses where several hundreds or thousands of people are found usually with small shops and limited services

(b) Four ways in which rural and urban settlements are interdependent:

(i) Rural settlements are sources of food for urban settlements

(i) Urban settlements are usually the sources of manufactured goods for the rural areas

(iii) Urban settlements are the sources of jobs or educational opportunities for the rural settlements

(iv) Urban settlements may also serve as administrative headquarters of the rural settlements

subject: Geography
q: In what ways can transportation contribute to the economic development of any one country?
explanation:
The contribution of transportation to the economic development of Nigeria

(i) It makes available to a community goods produced elsewhere

(ii) Transportation makes for regional specialisation in production. If goods are not moved, each region will produce less

(iii) Development of large cities depends on cheap and efficient transport system

(iv) It reduces the cost of goods to the consumers

(v) It reduces waste from over-production and stabilizes prices

(vi) it strengthens national unity as people from different parts of Nigeria easily interact with one another

(vii) It helps in national defense as troops can easily be moved to places where they are needed.

subject: Geography
q:
Write explanatory notes on any three of the following:

(a) Government's influence on the location of industries in Tropical Africa

(b) Nearness to market as a factor of industrial location in Tropical Africa

(c) The concentration of manufacturing industries n urban centres of developing countries

(d) The predominance of consumer oriented industries in developing countries
explanation:
(a) Government influences on the location of industries in tropical Africa. Government can directly or indirectly influence the location of industries. Such government influence may be owing to political, social, economic or military reasons. Government influences the location indirectly by offering incentives such as tax reliefs or low land rent, provision of basic amenities for the establishment of industries in an area.

(b) Nearness to market as a factor of industries location in Tropical Africa. Market is one of the factors affecting the location of industries. Industry will not be located where there is no market. Industries are located in areas of high population density because of the high purchasing power of the urban dwellers. Cost of transportation of manufactured goods is also reduced, and those industries whose finished products are bulkier than the raw materials are usually located at or near the market, e.g soft drinks and beer industries whose products are not only bulky but also breakable are located on or near the market.

(c) Concentration of manufacturing industries in urban centres of developing countries is due to

(i) Proximity to power, e.g electricity is easily available in urban areas

(ii) Supply of labour - due to high population in urban area, cheap labour can easily be available for the industries to use

(iii) Reduction in cost of transportation. Since there are good road network coupled with numerous vehicles, cost of transportation of both raw material and finished goods is reduced

(iv) Favourable market. Urban areas serve as good market for the location of industries because of high purchasing power of the urban dwellers

(iv) Easy source of capital: Most urban areas provide the means, i.e banks where entrepreneurs can easily get loan to boost their production

subject: Geography
q: What five factors determine the volume of trade between Nigeria and Ghana
explanation:
Five factors that determine the volume of trade between Nigeria and Ghana include:

(i) If the goods produced by both countries are not similar. If they are not similar, the volume of trade will increase, but if they are similar the volume of trade is going to be reduced.

(ii) Differences in climatic conditions: If there are differences in climatic conditions necessary for the production of certain crops, both countries will specialise in the production of different crops which will eventually lead to exchange of goods but if the climatic conditions are the same, both will specialise in the production of the same crops

(iii) The degree of industrial development: If one of the countries develops more industrially than the other, one will definitely demand for more manufactured goods than the other, hence exchange of goods (international trade)

(iv) Prices of goods offered: It the prices of goods offered by one of the countries is higher than what other countries may offer, the volume of trade will be increased but if the prices are low, one will expect a low volume of trade

(v) Foreign exchange earnings: If the foreign exchange earnings from both countries are high, then the volume of trade will increase but if the earnings are low the volume of trade will also be low

(a) On a sketch map of Nigeria locate and name any four vegetation belts

(b) Highlight four ways in which the forest vegetation contributes to the economy of the country

(c) Outline any four problems associated with the exploitation of the forest vegetation.
explanation:
(b) Four ways in which the forest vegetation contributes to the economy of the country are:

(i) Provision of timber for construction of houses and furniture

(ii) Provision of foreign exchange earnings from the export of timber 

(iii) Forest vegetation provides jobs to many people, e.g forest guards, lumbermen

(iv) Forest vegetation also provides medicinal herbs for the pharmaceutical industries.

(c) Four problems associated with exploitation of forest vegetation include:

(i) Heterogeneous nature of trees

(ii) Lack of motorable roads

(iii) High cost of survey to determine economic trees

(iv) Felling of timber is risky as many people have lost their lives in the process

subject: Geography
q:
(a) What is meant by the term hinterland?

(b) Highlight with specific examples, the importance of sea ports in the economy of Nigeria

(c) State any four problems of port development in Nigeria
explanation:
(a) Hinterland refers to the area of land that can be served by a particular sea port

(b) The importance of sea ports in the economy of Nigeria include:

(i) Sea ports like Apapa port serves as a point where imported and exported goods come in or move out of the country respectively

(ii) Sea ports generally like Port Harcourt and Apapa ports generate , revenue for the government

(iii) Sea ports generally provide employment for many people

(iv) Sea ports bring people together for the exchange of ideas and culture

(c) Four problems of port development in Nigeria are:

(i) The Deposition of sand, silt, clay at the delta and along the coast makes port development a problem

(ii) The nature of the coastline poses a problem as most coastlines are not suited for the development of ports

(iii) Shallow nature of the coasts; Most of the coasts in Nigeria are very shallow and this makes it difficult for large vessels to sail to the coast

(iv) Fluctuations in ocean tides: There is fluctuation in tides (rise in the volume of water in the ocean) along the coasts as there are occasions of high tides and other occasions of low tide Sokoto Plain

subject: Geography
q:
Contrast the Sokoto Plains with the Eastern highlands under the following headings

(a) relief and drainage

(b) resources and human activities

(c) problems of development
explanation:
Sokoto plain Eastern highlands

(a) Relief and drainage

 The relief is a lowland of less than 30m. Broken up by rivers Sokoto and Rima and their tributaries and their areas is usually flooded during the rainy season 
 

There are chains of hills along eastern border of Nigeria e.g Mambila, Biu Plateaux, Alantika, Shebshi mountains, Adamawa plateau. They range in altitude between 600m and 200m. The rivers that flow within this zone are Benue, Gongola, Taraba, kano, etc.

(b) Resources and human activities 

 Minerals found are limestone, gold, phosphate and gypsum. They are also involved in farming and rearing of cattle, sheep and goat, Industries are cement and local crafts  
 

Presence of minerals like limestone, iron ore and uranium. They are involved in agriculture, e.g cultivation of tea, coffee, yam, cassava, cattle, goat, sheep, etc and few industries like cement and textile.

(c) Problems of development

 (i) Inadequate supply of rainfall
(ii) Desert encroachment due to activities of man
(ii) Extensive land make communication very difficult 
 

 (i) Rugged relief causes remoteness and poor development of transportation
(ii) Problem of soil erosion and severe flooding
(iii) Cimatic variation due'to rainfall in some areas and none in other areas

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Draw a sketch map of Nigeria and on it, locate and name

(i) three major zones of modern manufacturing industries

(ii) two important towns within each zone

(b) Outline any three problems which result from the concentration of manufacturing industries in these zones

(c) Suggest three steps which can be taken to encourage the growth of industries outside these zones.
explanation:
(b) Three problems which result from the concentration of manufacturing industries in these zones are:

(i) Industrial and air pollution

(ii) Competition resulting in lower prices of goods

(iii) High pressure on existing social amenities like electricity, water, etc

(c) Three steps that can be taken to encourage the growth of industries outside these zones are:

(i) Provision of electricity, water, healthcare outside these zones

(ii) Granting of loans to entrepreneurs who want to build industries outside these zones

(iii) Granting of free land and long tax holiday to industries sited outside these zones.

subject: Geography
q:
Write explanatory notes on any four of the following

(a) Cocoa growing in western Nigeria

(b) Oil palm production in the eastern states

(c) Rice cultivation ire the middle belt

(d) Intensive farming in the Kano close-settled zone
explanation:
(a) Cocoa growing in Western Nigeria: Cocoa is cultivated in the Western Nigeria especially in Oyo, Ogun and Ondo States because cocoa grows best under certain conditions present in these areas which are undulating and well drained land, temperature of about 27°C, rainfall of 125-200cm, abundant sunshine for drying the beans, abundant labour to work in the cocoa farm and easy disposal of produce through sea ports. Crops sowed mature within five years. Harvested pods are crushed,beans fermented, dried and bagged for export.

(b) Oil palm production in the Eastern States: Oil palm is produced in the east like Owerri, Aba, Port Harcourt, etc. The crop grows best in temperature of not less than 21°C rainfall of 150-300cm, brilliant sunshine, deep loamy soil coupled with abundance of cheap labour. The crop is grown from the nuts, matures within 5-10 years and the matured fruits or bunch are harvested; processed and the products are palm oil and palm kernel which are used locally or exported.

(c) Rice cultivation in the Middle Belt: Rice is cultivated mainly as food crop for internal trade. Swamp rice is the type that is mainly grown in the middle belt of Nigeria such as Lafiagi, Badeggi and Pategi areas of the Niger valley. The seeds, are first raised in nursery and later transplanted to the Niger valley where the Niger has flooded its valley. They rice grows between 3-4 months after which it is harvested, processed and sold to local buyers.

(d) Intensive Farming in the Kano close - settled Zone; Intensive farming is practised in Kano as a result of high population. This high population results in land fragmentation hence farmers have to maximise the use of the small land through farming. During the farming seasons, they apply fertilizers and manure to promote the growth of crops and most of the crops grown are mainly cereals e.g millet, sorghum, maize

subject: Geography
q:
(a) On an outline map of West Africa, show
(i) one area having less than four months of rainfall annually
(ii) one area having rainfall all the year round
(iii) the position of the high pressure belt in January

(b) Give three reasons for the length of the rainy season in (a)(i) above

(c) Explain three problems that rainfall may pose to agriculture in (a) (ii) above
explanation:
(b) Three reasons for the length of rainy season in the area having less than four months or rain annually.

(i) Long distance from the ocean. The area is too far from the ocean

(ii) Nearness to the desert. The area is very close to the sahara desert, hence it experiences low rainfall.

(iii) Effects of trade winds. The N. E trade winds tend to have more influence on the area than the S.E trade winds.

(c) Three problems due tc continuous rainfall all the year round are:

(i) Soil erosion: Too much rain results in soil erosion

(ii) Leaching: Too much rain leads to loss of nutrients from the surface of the soil

(iii) Poor yield of crops due to either damage to the crop or lack of sufficient sunshine to produce fruits.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) On an outline map of West Africa, locate and name
(i) two ECOWAS member countries along the coast
(ii) two ECOWAS member countries in the interior
(iii) the capital of each of the countries

(b) Describe six benefits which any one of the countries shown on your map derives from its membership of the ECOWAS
explanation:
(b) Six benefits Liberia derives from its membership of the ECOWAS:

(i) There has been increase in trade between Liberia and other ECOWAS members

(ii) Liberia was once involved in civil war but is now a peaceful country through the effort of ECOMOG

(iii) Free movement of Liberians within the sub-region has fostered unity and cooperation between Liberia and others

(iv) Road development between Liberia and her neighbours has helped in the transport network of Liberia

(v) Border dispute between Liberia and Sierra Leone has been settled by ECOWAS members

(vi) Liberia's participation in ECOWAS games held in Lagos in 1977 aided understanding and solidarity among the states.

subject: Geography
q:

(i) one area each in a different country, important for diamond, petroleum and gold
(ii) one important town in each area
(iii) one route from the producing area to the port of export for each mineral

(b) Describe one method of mining any one of the minerals in (a) above

(c) Highlight four problems associated with mining in West Africa
explanation:
(b) One method of mining petroleum is drilling:

(i) The underground rocks are sampled and tested for traces of oil

(ii) When oil is discovered, a well is sunk to reach it by the help of a drilling rig or derrick

(iii) The oil is then pumped out

(iv) A system of valves called "Christmas tree" is created to control the flow into tanks

(c) Four problems associated with mining in West Africa; 

(i) Lack of fund

(ii) Lack of technical know-how

(iii) Absence of minerals

(iv) Lack of sources of efficient and reliable power

(ii) For fishing

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Of what importance is the Aswan High Dam to the Nile Basin?

(b) Identify four problems resulting from the construction of the dam.
explanation:
(a) Economic importance of Aswan High Dam to the Nile Basin are:

(i) Generation of Hydro-electric power (electricity)

(ii) Aswan dam also serves irrigation purposes. This enables the people to farm throughout the year

(iii) Dam is also used as fishing ground

(iv) Aswan dam also provides source of water for domestic and industrial uses

(v) Aswan dam also serves as tourist centre where revenue can be generated.

(b) Four problems resulting from the construction of dams are:

(i) There may be low level of water in the river which may hamper navigation and other activities

(ii) The collapse of the dam may lead to flooding of the plain which may cause damage to life and properties

(iii) Huge amount of money which would have been utilized in some useful projects is lost through the construction of dam

(iv) Shortage of water in the river during dry season may hamper the normal functioning of the dam.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Write explanatory notes on the
(i) Equinoxes
(ii) Great circles

(b) Explain why the period of twilight is longer along the Arctic Circle than at the Equator
explanation:
(a) (i) Equinoxes: Refers to two particular days of the year when all parts of the world will experience equal days and nights. These two days are March 21st and September 23rd. During these two days, the sun is vertically overhead at the equator and all parts of the earth will experience equal days and equal nights.

(ii) Great Circles: Any circle which divides a globe into two equal parts (or hemispheres) is a great circle. Examples are the equator, Greenwich meridian, meridian 180°, meridians 10°E and 170°W. 50°E and 130°W. etc. are all great circles. The number of great circles is limitless. It can extend east to west, north to south and so on provided it divides the globe into hemispheres.

(b) The reason(s) why the period of twilight is longer along the Arctic circle than at the Equator; 
The brief period between sun set and complete darkness is called twilight. This is caused by the fact that during the period of twilight, the earth receives diffused or refracted light from the sun while it is still below the horizon. Since the sunrises and sets in a vertical path at the Equator, the period during which refracted light is received is short. But at the arctic circle, the sun rises and sets in an obligue path and the period of refracted light is longer. It is much longer still at the poles.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Distinguish between deflation and abrasion

(b) Using specific examples, describe one landform produced by each of the processes ill (a) above

(c) Give any two reasons why wind erosion is more active in arid than in humid regions
explanation:
(a) The differences between deflation and abrasion are as follows;
Deflation involves the lifting and blowing away of loose materials from the ground. Such unconsolidated sands and pebbles may be carried in the air or rolled along the ground depending on the size while abrasion involves the sand blasting of rock surfaces by winds when they hurl sand particles against them. The effect of sand blasting results in rock surfaces being scratched, polished and worn away.

(b) One landform produced by deflation is the deflation hollows. Deflation results in the lowering of the land surface gradually until large depressions are formed called deflation hollows. One landform produced by abrasion is the Rock pedestals. The sand blasting effect of winds against any projecting rock masses wears back the soft layers so that an irregular edge is formed on the alternate bands of hard and soft rocks. Grooves and hollows are cut in the rock surfaces, carving them into fantastic pillars called rock pedestals.

(c) Two reasons why wind erosion is more active in arid than in humid regions.

(i) In arid regions like the desert, wind erosion is more active because of absence of vegetation. Absence of vegetation enables the-wind to move at high speed thereby causing wind erosion.

(ii) Absence of moisture to bind the soil particles together. Since there is no moisture, the soil particles are loosely held hence they can easily be carried away by winds.

(a) (i) Name any three of Koppen's major climatic groups
(ii) What is the basis of this type of classification?

(b) Name any two types of climate within Koppen's first group and show the unique features of each type.
explanation:

(i) Those based on climate and vegetation

(ii) Those based on temperature

(iii) Those based on precipitation-evaporation ratios.

(ii) The basis of Koppen's classification is vegetation.

(b) Two types of climate within Koppen's first group and their features are:

(i) The moist and Hot climate; features; 

(i) Tropical rainy climate

(ii) Average monthly temperature above 18°C

(iii) Climate has no winter seasons

(iv) Annual rainfall is large and exceeds annual evaporation

(ii) The moist and warm climate: features:

(i) Coldest month has an average temperature of under 18°C

(ii) Has above —3°C in at least one month

(iii) Has an average temperature of above 10°C

(iv) It has both summer and winter seasons.

subject: Geography
q:
Choose any two of the following vegetation types

(a) Equatorial

(b) Tropical continental

(c) Tropical desert and for each
(i) describe the vegetation characteristics
(ii) Explain how the vegetation has been influenced by climate
explanation:
Two of the vegetation types are;
(a) Equatorial
(b) Tropical desert

(i) Characteristics of Equatorial vegetation are:

(i) The trees are evergreen due to absence of dry season.

(ii) The trees are mainly tall and broad leafed

(iii) The trees are mainly tropical hardwood

(iv) Trees are heterogeneous (different species)

(v) There are three distinct layers of the forest upper, middle and lower storeys

(vi) Lots of undergrowth e.g climbers, epiphytes, ropes, etc

(ii) The climatic conditions which influence the vegetation are:

(i) Great uniformity of temperatures throughout the year

(ii) The annual range of temperature is not more than 30°C

(iii) High mean monthly temperature, which is always around 26°C

(iv) Very heavy rainfall of 150 — 200cm, per annum which is well distributed.

(i) Characteristics of tropical desert are:

(i) Presence of combination of plants — grass, shrubs, herbs, roots etc.

(ii) Presence of xerophytes (drought resistant shrubs) e.g bulbous cacti

(iii) Presence of halophytes (salt loving plants)

(iv) Plants possess long roots with little or no leaves

(v) some possess thick succulent stems.

(vi) some shed their leaves during dry season

(ii) How the vegetation is influenced by climate; 

(i) Temperature is very high (30°C - 58°C)

(ii) Mean annual temperature is 32°C

(iii) Annual range of temperature is 9°C.

(iv) Rainfall is very low (25cm per annum)

subject: Geography
q:
Select either urban flooding or water pollution and discuss its

(a) causes

(b) effects

(c) control measures
explanation:
(a) Causes of urban flooding:

(i) Abundant supply of water from rainfall or ice

(ii) High water table which ensures a steady water supply

(iii) Weak dam construction and embarkments

(iv) Poor drainage system

(v) Blocking of drainage systems e.g gutters

(vi) Unplanned urban settlements

(b) Effects of urban flooding:

(i) Human lives are lost during urban flooding

(ii) Properties are damaged

(iii) Some roads may be washed away or rendered impassable

(iv) Some houses are completely taken over by water during flooding

(v) Deposits of garbage left behind by flood can cause environmental pollution.

(c) Control measure of urban flooding:

(i) Construction of strong embankments along the banks of rivers

(ii) Retardation of run off over the surface of water sheds

(iii) Storage of flood water behind dams to serve as flood control reservoirs

(iv) Stream channelization

(v) Legislation against indiscrimate dumping of refuse in gutters.

(vi).Construction of functional drainage systems.

subject: Geography

(a) Explain the term environmental resource

(b) Using specific examples, describe the importance of water as an environmental resource

(c) Outline any three sources of water.
explanation:
(a) Environmental resource is any resource within our environment which is useful to man

(b) The importance of water as an environmental resource include the following:

(i) Rain water enables our crops to grow

(ii) Water from rivers can be used for construction of hydro-electric power supply (H. E. P)

(iii) Rainwater and water from rivers can be used for domestic and industrial uses

(iv) Rivers and seas provide employment for many people e.g fishermen

(v) Rivers, ponds, lakes, oceans provide food in form of protein to man e.g fish, shrimps crabs, etc

(vi) Rivers and ocean can be used for tourist centres

(c) Three sources of water:
(i) Rain water
(ii) Water from rivers
(iii) Water from the ground (ponds or wells)

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Name any four functions of towns giving a specific example of each

(b) In what four ways can the initial function of a town contribute to its growth?

(c) Highlight any three ways in which rural settlements are dependent on urban settlements.
explanation:
(a) Four functions of towns include:

(i) Industrial function

(ii) Commercial function

(iii) Administrative function

(iv) Residential function

(b) Four ways the initial function of a town contribute to its growth:

(i) As a result of the siting of many industries in a town due to large market, easy transportation, availability of power; people tend to migrate to the town to take up employment in the industries

(ii) Also, as a result of commercial activities in the town, people tend to settle in the town resulting in the growth of that town.

(iii) Due to the fact that the seat of government is located there, people tend to move closer to the government resulting in the growth of the town

(iv) The availability of social amenities such as pipe-borne water, electricity, etc, people tend to move into the town to enjoy these amenities resulting in its growth.

(c) Three ways in which rural settlements are dependent on urban settlements include:

(i) Urban settlements are usually the sources of manufactured goods for the rural settlements.

(ii) Urban settlements may also serve as administrative headquarters of the rural settlements

(iii) It is also a source of employment to rural settlements

subject: Geography
q: In what ways can poor transportation limit the economic development of any one country?
explanation:
Ways in which poor transportation can limit the economic development of a country e.g Niger Republic include:

(i) Poor transportation hinders easy movement of goods from one part of the country to another

(ii) It tends to reduce specialisation in production because there are no ways produce can easily move out or come into the country

(iii) Poor transportation lowers economic development because of lack of linkages of areas of production with consumption centres

(iv) It reduces trade between towns and countries

(v) It also lowers international integration and peace

(vi) Poor transportation hinders diffusion of ideas and innovations.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Account for the relative low level of industrial development in tropical Africa

(b) State five ways in which the industrial sector has contributed to the economic development of the region
explanation:
(a) Reasons for the relatively low level of industrial development in tropical Africa are:

(i) Inadequate capital. Entrepreneurs in Africa do not have large capital to establish industries

(ii) Inadequate skilled manpower. There is serious shortage of skilled labour especially in management and technical staff

(iii) Small market The existence of small market (i.e consumers) prevents the growth of industries in Africa

(iv) Inadequate infrastructure -electricity, pipe borne water, telecommunication, roads, etc, are lacking hence a low growth of industries in Africa

(v) Shortage of raw-materials - Raw materials are not adequately available locally hence some depend on imported raw materials. This lowers industrial growth.

(b) Five contributions of industrial sector to the economic development of the region include:

(i) It leads to the diversification of economies

(ii) It has helped to conserve foreign exchange that would have been used in the importation of goods

(iii) Industrial sector provides employment to many people

(iv) It also raises the general standard of living of the people

(v) Industrial goods exported also generate foreign exchange to the country.

(a) (i) Rainfall has a lot of effects on agricultural activities in the tropical continental region of Nigeria in the following ways:

(i) Rainfall is low in this region

(ii) As a result of low rainfall, it gives rise to savanna type of vegetation

(iii) Agricultural activities in this area include the cultivation of cereal crops like maize, rice, millet sorghum, etc.

(ii)  on sub-equatorial region of Nigeria

(i) Rainfall is very high in the sub-equatorial region of Nigeria 

(ii) This high rainfall gives rise to rainforest type of vegetation

(iii) Agricultural activities in this re igo n involve the growth of tree crops like cocoa, kola, oil palm, rubber, etc

(iv) Due to lack of grasses, livestock is not popular.

(v) Timber is present hence people involve in lumbering activities.

(ii) It provides raw materials for industries

(iii) It provides employment to many people

(iv) It generates foreign exchange for the country

(b) Discuss the relationship between the pressure belts an rainfall distribution in West Africa.
explanation: (b) There is direct relationship between pressure belts and rainfall distribution in Vest Africa. Pressure belts are created by temperature. When the temperature of a place is very high, it creates a high pressure belt. Wind which carries a lot of moisture therefore blows from a region of high pressure belt to regions of low pressure belts. This therefore results in heavy rainfall in areas of low pressure belts and light rainfall in areas of high pressure belts.

(iii) Period of seasons, long dry season tends to reduce the volume of water in the rivers.

subject: Geography
q:
With the aid of suitable diagrams, describe the characteristics and mode of formation of any two of the following

(a) Ox-bow lake

(b) Zeugen

(c) Block mountain
explanation:
Characteristics and mode of formation of block mountain. Block mountain is formed from faulting and it involves the following:

(i) Two parallel faults develop

(ii) The block enclosed by the two parallel faults remains as it is

(iii) The land on either side of the parallel faults subsides

(iv) The enclosed centre-block, now up standing becomes the horst or block mountain 

(b) Characteristics and mode of formation of Zengen: Zengen is formed as a result of wind abrasion. ​​​​​Zengens are formed in areas in the desert which have a surface layer to hard rock over-lying another layer of soft rock. Before wind abrasion can produce Zengen, fissures or cracks opened by very high temperature on the desert surface must have occurred on the hard surface layer. Wind abrasion then attacks the desert surface through the fissures already existing. The fissures are widened to become furrows while the blocks between the fissures (now furrows) become ridges called "Zengens"

 (iv) Contains lots of deposits such as oozes clay, etc

(c) The economic importance of continental shelves man;

(i) Continental shelves are the richest fishing grounds in the world due to the abundance of planktons (fish food). These planktons are numerous because of the shallow-ness of the continental shell which enable sunlight to penetrate to the bottom of shelves.

(ii) It provides employment to many people, fishermen

(iii) Development of seaports. This is due the shallow nature of the continental shelves.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) What is soil?

(b) Explain the process of soil formation

(c) Describe a typical soil profile
explanation:
(a) Soil is defined as the uppermost layer of the earth crust in which plants get supports and food (or nutrient. 

(b) The process of soil formation is called weathering. Weathering is defined as the breaking down of rocks into tir pieces to form soil

(c) The process of weathering involves three processes:

(i) Physical weathering

(ii) Chemical weathering

(iii) Biological weathering

(i) Physical Weathering: This involves the breaking down of rocks into tin pieces by special agents such as temperature, running water, ice,wind, pressure, etc.

(ii) Chemical Weathering; This involves the breaking down of rocks into tiny pieces by chemical means such as solution, oxidation, carbonation hydrolysis and hydration.

(iii) Biological weathering: Involves the breaking down of rocks by biological agent suc as earthworm, roots of plants, hooves of animals, rodents and the activities of man.

(c) Soil Profile: A vertical section cut across the soil to show the arrangement of layers is called soil profile. The different horizontal layers are called horizons and there are 3 main horizons in a well-weathered soil namely.
A - horizon
B - horizon and
C - horizon.

A Horizon: This is the top soil. It contains remains of plants and anima which have decomposed to form humus, it is also known as the alluvial horizon which is derived by the washing c clay and fine soil particles down the soil profile.

B - Horizon: This is zone of enrichment (alluvial horizon or accumulatic of soil minerals). The minerals have been washed down from the A- horizon and converted into inorganic compound It is regarded as the sub-soil

C - Horizon: This is the layer of underlying weathered debris. It is the source of pares material. It in underlain by hard unweathered rock.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Name four components of the ecosystem

(b) How does the carbon cycle explain the inter-dependence of the components in the ecosystem?
explanation:
(a) Four components of the ecosystem are:

(i) Lithosphere (solid-earth crust)

(ii) Hydrosphere (liquid component)

(iii)Atmosphere (gaseous component)

(iv) Biosphere (Living organisms component).

(b) Carbon cycles have helped to explain the inter-dependence of the components in the ecosystem in the following ways.

(i) There is the inter-relationship between plants and the atmosphere as it is from the atmosphere that plan. derive the carbon dioxide that they need for photosynthesis. Then man and other animals give out carbon dioxide through the process of respiration.

(ii) There is an inter-dependence within the biotic components themselves. The herbivores together with the carnivores and decomposers, pass on the energy and matter

(iii) Man also contribute to the carbon in the atmosphere through the burning of fossil fuel, e.g coal, petroleum, liquid gas and burning of wood

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Account for the rapid growth of world population in the last two decades

(b) Explain any three problems arising from this rapid growth

(c) Suggest solutions to these problems
explanation:
(a) Reasons for rapid-growth of world population in the last two decades are:

(i) Modernization of agriculture leading to plentiful and varied food supply

(ii) Improved health services

(iii) Knowledge of the cause of infectious diseases and how to prevent them

(iv) Better sanitation

(v) Reduced infant mortality rate as a result of better care

(vi) A lowering of the marriage age from 22 to 20 and below

(vii) Change in child bearing pattern as women want to have all their children at once and have a rest later

(viii) Modernization and industrialization make standard of living to improve.

(b) Three problems arising from this rapid growth; 

(i) Problem of housing (accommodation)

(ii) Problem of transportation

(iii) Shortage of food (or starvation)

(c) Solutions to these problems; 

(i) Government and other agencies should establish residential estates

(ii) More roads, railways and other transportation network to take care of the increasing population

(iii) Mechanized agriculture should be practised.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Differentiate clearly between urban settlements and rural settlements

(b) Explain four factors responsible for tl-increase in size of urban settlements

(c) Highlight four problems associated with such growth.
explanation:
(a) Urban settlement is a relatively large, dense and permanent settlement of socially heterogeneous individuals usually found in towns, cities or megalopolis while rural settlement is a relatively small and scattered settlement of socially and culturally homogenous individuals found in villages or hamlets.

(b) Four factors responsible for the increase in size of urban settlement include:

(i) Availability of jobs (or employments)

(ii) Presence of social amenities, e.g electricity, water

(iii) Presence of administrative headquarters in urban area

(iv) Involvement of people in commercial and industrial activities in urban areas

(c) Four problems associated with urban growth:

(i) Inadequate transport facilities

(ii) Undue increase in human and vehicular traffic

(iii) Overuse and deterioration of available social services

(iv) increase in the rate of crimes.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Explain, giving examples, four ways in which physical factors may hinder the development of road transportation

(b) Discuss any four factors responsible for the poor network of railways in tropical African countries
explanation:
(a) Four ways in which physical factors may hinder the development of road transportation are:

(i) Rugged highlands create pattern for the development of roads

(ii) Climatic factors like rainfall can wash away existing roads or make it more difficult to construct new ones

(iii) Rivers, especially large ones do contribute greatly to the development of roads as huge amount is spent in the construction and erection of bridges

(iv) Swampy area is also a problem as it also takes large amount of money to construct bridges and flyovers across them.

(b) Four factors responsible for the poor network of railways in tropical African countries include:

(i) Capital costs for maintenance and operation of rail transport are usually high

(ii) Lack of spare parts and technical know-how tend to reduce the rail transportation

(iii) Rail lines are constructed only around mineral or agricultural producing areas hence the absence of these products results in low development of rail

(iv) it is relatively too slow and people do not want to travel with it.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Describe three ways in which light industries differ from heavy industries

(b) Give four reasons why light industries are more prevalent than heavy industries in tropical Africa.
explanation:
(a) Three differences between light industries and heavy industries 

Light industries  Heavy industries

(i) They produce relatively small light weight goods, e.g shoes
(ii)-The products of light industries are mainly final or consumer goods
(iii) It usually employs a lot of female workers 

(i) They produce heavy and bulky goods, e,g iron and steel
(ii) Products are not final nor consumer good rather they are used in construction or manufacturing industries
(iii) It employs male workers due to the nature of work involved 

(b) Four reasons why light industries are more prevalent than heavy industries in tropical Africa are:

(i) Lack of capital; Poor funding leads to the establishment of light industries than heavy industries

(ii) Lack of technical know-how: Africans do not have the skills for heavy industrial operations

(iii) Lack of raw materials; Raw materials: for heavy, industries are lacking in African countries

(iv) Africans generally tend to engage in production of consumable goo than non-consumable goods because of the low level of development.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Give four reasons why countries engage in world trade 

(b) How have these reasons affected the volume of trade between Nigeria and her neighbouring countries?

(C)(i) Name any two sea routes through which Nigeria trades with the outside world
(ii) Indicate one commodity that Nigeria engages exports through each route
explanation:
(a) Four reasons why countries engage in world trade:

(i) Uneven distribution of natural resources, e.g minerals

(ii) Differences in climate resulting in production of different crops in different countries

(iii) The need to generate foreign exchange

(iv) Differences in technical know-how and expertise for the production of mechanised goods a electronics.

(b) (i) Minerals which are not found in Nigeria are imported from neighbouring countries while Nigeria exports her minerals like petroleum to others which do not have it

(ii) Due to differences in climate, some crops which cannot be grown in Nigeria are imported while those that can be grown are exported. But because Nigeria a her neighbours have almost the same climate, the volume of trade tends to be reduced

(iii) There is reduction of t volume of trade between Nigeria and her neighbours because they do not have the technical know-how to produce goods

(iii) Foreign exchange earnings between Nigeria and her neighbours is also very low because they produce almost the same types of goods or products.

(c)(i) Two sea routes through which Nigeria trades with outside wo are:

(a) South Atlantic route and

(b) The Cape route

(c)(ii) The commodity is petroleum (or crude oil)

subject: Geography

(i) one inland drainage basin
(ii) three rivers that flow into the basin
(iii) the Bight of Benin and Bight of Bonny
(iv) the Idanre Hill, Oban Hills and Biu plateau

(b) Account for the shallowness of the rivers and the lake of the inland drainage basin (c) Describe any four benefits of the lake.
explanation:
(b) Reasons for the shallowness of rivers and lakes of the inland drainage basin are:

(i) Water from these rivers always flows into Lake Chad without flowing out

(ii) Silt and other weathered rock materials continue to drop into the lakes

(iii) There is the high evaporation of water in the rivers and the lakes.

(c) Four benefits of the lake include:

(i) For irrigation purposes

(ii) For fishing

(iii) For transportation of goods and people

(iv) For domestic and industrial uses, e.g drinking

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Distinguish between internal trade and international trade

(b) Name four commodities of internal trade in Nigeria, - two originating from the north and two from the south

(c) Identify the factors that promote trade between the northern and southern parts of Nigeria

(d) What are the limitations to internal trade in Nigeria?
explanation:
(a) Internal trade involves the trade in goods within country while international trade is the type of tad( done between one country and another

(b) Four commodities of internal trade

(i) From the north are
(a) Cattle and
(b) Groundnut

(ii) From the south a
(a) Palm oil and
(b) Kola

(c) Factors that promote trade between Southern and Northern Nigeria are:

(i) Differences in the production of certain goods. Goods produced in the North are different from goods produce in the south, hence the need to exchange goods

(ii) Efficient transportation. e.g road, railway, etc.

(iii) Use of the same currency

(iv) Favourable price attached to the commodities involved in internal trade

(d) Limitations of internal trade:

(i) Long distance between point of production and point of sales

(ii) Language barrier

(iii) High cost of transportation

(iv) Perishability of products.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Explain any two of the following methods of farming as adopted in Nigeria
(i) Mixed farming
(ii) Intensive farming
(iii) Plantation agriculture

(b) State three problems facing agriculture in Nigeria

(c) In what ways are these problems being solved?
explanation:
(a)(i) Mixed farming; is the type of farming in which farmers grow crops and also rear animals on the sal piece of land. Farmers grow certain crops and rear animals on the same land.

(ii) Plantation agriculture; involves the cultivation of a single crop over a large area of land. Modern implements like tractor, plop harrow, planters, etc, are used in plantation agriculture In most cases, cash crops especially tree crops a grown in plantation agriculture and the produce an mainly for sale.

(b) Three problems facing agriculture in Nigeria are:

(i) Lack of finance or credit facilities.

(ii) Inadequate land due to land tenure system

(iii) Lack of storage and processing facilities.

(c) The problem above can be solved as follows:

(i) Government ce easily grant loans to farmers

(ii) Land should be rn available to prospective farmers through the land decree of 1978

(iii) Government should provide storage and processing facilities at reduced rate.

subject: Geography

(b) Name any one crop that is grown in each of the zones shown on your map

(c) Describe the soil characteristics that have favoured the cultivation of the type of crops in each zone.
explanation:
(b) Soil characteristics that have favoured the cultivation of cocoa (Zones C):

(i) The land is undulating

(ii) It has well drained surface

(iii) It has a deep clayey and rich-in-acid soil.

Soil characteristics that favoured the cultivation of groundnut (Zone A):

(i) The soil has a wide plain

(ii) The top soil (A - Horizon) is humus

(iii) The soil is loose and sandy.

Soil characteristics that favoured the cultivation of yam (ZoneB):

(i) Well drained soil

(ii) Soil is sandy loam

(iii) It is not acidic and it is well aerated.

subject: Geography

(b) Highlight the significance of the concentration to the inhabitants of the area.

Four factors that encourage concentration of industries

(i) Nearness to power

(ii) Availability of labour

(iii) Availability of good roads

(ix) Nearness to market

(i) It leads to the production of cheaper goods

(ii) It ensures specialisation

(iv) It leads to exchange of ideas

subject: Geography
q:
(a) On a sketch map of West Africa, show and name (i) the home areas of the Ashanti, the Wolof and the Mandingo
(ii) two areas with a population density of over 100 persons per km2
(iii) two areas with a population density of less than 5 persons per km2

(b) Explain how the variation in population density in West Africa has been aided by the following: (i) Historical factors (11) Agriculture (iii) Mining (iv) Industrialisation
explanation:
(b)(i) Historical factors: These have contributed to the variation in the population density in West Africa because ancient cities that developed like Kano, Katsina, Timbuktu are due to trans-saharan trade which took place several years ago. The population of these areas grew because of natural defence during war and being the sites of traditional empires those days

(ii) Agricultural activities tend to have possible effects on population distribution e.g the eastern Nigeria, part of Ashanti developed with high population because of intensive agricultural activities due to fertility of the soil, but where the land is not fertile, people tend to abandon such areas

(iii) Mining; The presence of minerals in certain areas tends to attract high population because of availability of jobs and other facilities in the areas

(iv) Industrialisation. People tend to move to places where there are many industries , as these will help to provide job and improve the standard of living of the people.

(c) Describe three ways in which the rivers of Africa are of importance.
explanation:

(ii) Minerals are found in highlands

(iii) Highlands serve as sources of rivers

(c) Three importance of African rivers:

subject: Geography
q:
(a) On a sketch map of Africa, mark and name:
(i) one area receiving rainfall throughout the year
(ii) one area receiving rainfall in summer only
(iii) one warm ocean current (indicating the direction of flow)
(iv) one cold ocean current (indicating the direction of flow)

(b) (i) Identify the type of nature vegetation found in each of the areas shown on your map
(ii) How has rainfall accounted for the difference in vegetation in the two areas?
explanation:
(b)(i) The type of vegetation found in:

(1) /Area wi rainfall is throughout the year is equatorial forest

(ii) The type of vegetation found in areas of summer rainfall is savanna grassland

(iii) As a result of continuous rainfall, the vegetation becomes evergreen with tall trees. The trees are of different layers with canopies. But due to rainfall only in summer the trees are deciduous. i.e the trees shed the leaves during the dry season so as to conserve sufficient water. Hence most of the plants found are mainly grasses.

subject: Geography
q:
Give reasons for each of any three of the following:

(a) the importance of fruit farming in the Mediterranean lands

(b) the predominance of lumbering in the Zaire basin

(c) the declining importance of bush fallowing in WestAfrica

(d) the importance of plantation agriculture in EastAfrica.
explanation:
(a) Importance of fruit farming in the Mediterranean lands include:

(i) Fruits like oranges, bananas, pineapples are consumed in many homes and are also processed into various forms of juices

(ii) Most of the fruits produced are for export to European market thereby generating foreign exchange

(iii) It also provides employment opportunities to people

(iv) Provides raw materials for canning and processing industries

(v) Wine is obtained from processing of vines which are produced in the Mediterranean lands.

(b) The predominance of lumbering in Zaire basin.

(i) The dense equatorial forest is a source of valuable timber (50%) of Zaire is forested)

(ii) River Zaire and its numerous tributaries provide means of transporting the logs to sawmills or to ports for export

(iii) There is ready market for the sawn timber both internally and externally

(iv) There is high demand for timber as fuel

(c) The importance of plantation agriculture in East Africa

(i) Local people learn new skills in the job as machine operators, seed planters etc

(ii) It provides employment opportunities to the people

(iii) Plantation agriculture helps in bringing about general development in the area where it is practised

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Outline any three achievements of the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) since its formation

(b) What five problems are facing the ECOWAS in the achievement of its objectives?
explanation:
(a) Three achievements of ECOWAS since its formation

(i) There is now free movement of people between the countries as travel visas for visits not lasting more than three months were abolished in 1979

(ii) Border dispute between Benin and Togo was resolved in the spirit of ECOWAS in 1975

(iii) The ECOWAS games held in Lagos in 1977 aided understanding and solidarity among the states

(b) Five problems facing ECOWAS in the achievement of its objectives

(i) The division of West African states along linguistic lines (English and French) discourages the coming together of the two groups.

(ii) Many of the states are unable to pay their annual dues. This makes the running of the secretariat difficult.

(iii) The smaller and poor countries fear that the bigger and richer ones will dominate them economically and politically

(iv) Some of the richer countries are also suspicious that their resources might be tapped to enrich poorer countries

(v) The countries as they become independent adopted their own different currencies. This has complicated trade

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Highlight any three effects of either rotation or revolution of the earth

(b) Give reasons why:
(i) all areas north of the Arctic Circle have twenty four hours of daylight on June 21
(ii) areas within the tropics have more sunshine than areas outside the tropics
explanation:
(a) Three effects of rotation of the earth include:

(i) It causes day and night

(ii) It causes the deflection of winds and ocean currents

(iii) It causes the rising and falling of the tides.

(b)(i) Reasons why all areas north of the Arctic cycle have 24 hours of daylight on June 21:

(i) The sun appears to move northwards on June 21

(ii) Daylight increases as one goes towards the north pole.

(iii) The sun never sets at mid-summer (nature of season)

(iv) The earth is inclined to the plane of the ecliptic which causes the variation in length of daylights.

(b)(ii) Reasons why areas within the tropics have more sunshine than areas outside the tropics

(i) The sun is always overhead at the tropics

(ii) It represents the centre of the earth

(iii) It is the region which only experiences equal day and night (equinoxes)

(iv) There is variation in length of day and night towards the poles.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Using appropriate diagrams, explain the process of formation of a
(i) river capture
(ii) waterfall

(b) Describe one way in which each of the two features above is useful to man
explanation:
(a)(i) River Capture: This is also called river piracy or river beheading. It results when a river through head water of erosion into a water divide cuts up the headwater of another but a weaker river. The river pirated is called misfit and the point of capture is called elbow of capture The valley below the elbow is called wind gap. 

(i) Waterfall: This is formed as a result of unequal resistance of hard and soft rocks transverse by a river. The out crop of a band of hard rock may cause a river to fall downstream in greater dimension from some heights called waterfall 

(b) One way in which river capture is useful to man is channelization of water away from settlements while waterfall is useful for the generation of hydro-electric power

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Distinguish between an ecosystem and a food chain

(b) Explain the interdependence of the biotic and abiotic components of the environment
explanation:
(a) Ecosystem; is defined as the relationship between the living things and the non-living things in a community. The living things are referred to as the biotic factors while the non-living things are the abiotic factors. Examples of biotic factors are plants and animals which can be producers or consumers, while the abiotic factors include temperature, wind, rainfall, sunlight, etc. Food chain refers to the linear feeding relationships among certain species of a community. Food chain can lead to energy transfer from one organism to another. Examples of a food chain is

Grass →Grasshoppers →Toads→ Hawks
Grass is the producer while grasshopper, toad and hawks are consumers 

(b) The interdependence of the biotic and abiotic components of the environment; 

(i) Plants (biotic) depend on oxygen (abiotic) for respiration

(ii) Plants (biotic) also depend on carbon dioxide (non biotic) for photosynthesis

(iii) Plants (biotic) also depend on water (abiotic) for nutrition and other uses

(iv) Plants also require sunlight for photosynthesis

(v) Abiotic factors like oxygen, carbon dioxide are produced by plants and animals during photosynthesis and respiration respectively.

subject: Geography
q:
Write explanatory notes on each of the following
(a) deforestation
(b) drought
explanation:
(a) Deforestation: This is the process of cutting down trees or forest for the purpose of farming, lumbering, mining, grazing, etc without immediate replanting of trees.

Effects of deforestation include:

(i) It exposes the soil to rain and wind erosion

(ii) It destroys the natural forest which is rich in natural resources

(iii) It could lead to flooding and desertification.

Control include

(i) Afforestation: which is the planting of new trees

(ii)Avoiding over-grazing

(iii)Avoiding indiscriminate bush burning

(iv) Use of forest guards.

(b) Drought: This is also called dryness. It occurs when there is-no rainfall or when it is inadequate with increase in temperature. It causes heat and dusty environment, poor yield of crops and desert encroachment. Control is by irrigation, mulching of the ground to conserve water, planting of trees, creation of shelter belts, etc

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Explain the term migration and describe any three forms it may take

(b) State any four factors that may be responsible for migration

(c) Outline any three advantages and three disadvantages of any one type of migration mentioned in (a) above.
explanation:
(a) Migration is the movement of people from one geographical area tc another involving permanent or temporary settlement.

Three forms of migration are:

(i) Rural-Urban migration

(ii) Urban-Rural migration

(iii) Urban-Urban migration

(b) Four factors that may be responsible for migration include:

(i) Physical conditions like climate, relief, etc

(ii) Natural disasters like famine, floods, etc

(iii) Insecurity e.g war

(iv) Differences in the availability of social amenities

(c) Three advantages or Rural-Urban Migration:

(i) reduced population pressure on agricultural lands

(ii) Supply of migrant labour

(iii) Promotion of cultural integration.

Three disadvantages of Rural-Urban Migration:

(i) It leads to unemployment

(ii) It leads to high cost of living

(iii) There is pressure on social amenities.

subject: Geography
q: Highlight the relative contributions of roads and railways to national development in any country of your choice
explanation:
3. The relative contributions of roads and railways to national development in U.S.A

(i) Increased agricultural,mineral and industrial production

(ii) It aids the movement of people from one place to another

(iii) It links areas of production with areas of consumption

(iv) It leads to increased trade between towns

(v) It generates revenue

(vi) It leads to diffusion of ideas and innovations

(vii) It promotes employment opportunities.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Explain the term manufacturing industry

(b) Highlight the characteristic features of manufacturing industries in Tropical African countries

(c) Give any four reasons why countries embark on industrialization
explanation:
(a) Manufacturing industry involves the turning of raw materials into new products by mechanical or chemical processes at home or in the factory.

(b) The characteristic features of manufacturing industries in tropical African countries:

(i) Importation of skilled labour

(ii) Imported raw materials

(iii)They are labour intensive

(iv) They are largely small-scaled

(v) The industries are concentrated in few locations

(c) Four reasons why countries embark on industrialization include:

(i) To reduce over dependence on imported goods

(ii) It acts as a source of revenue

(iii) To generate employment

(iv) To improve the standard of living.

subject: Geography
q: Compare and contrast the trade relations between Nigeria and the United Kingdom with that between Nigeria and Japan
explanation:
(a) Trade between Nigeria and United Kingdom and that between Nigeria and Japan.

Similarities:
(i) There is importation of finished products from both countries

(ii) There is export of primary products to both countries

(iii) There is importation of skilled labour from both countries

(iv) There is importation of technology from both countries.

Differences:
(i) There is older trade relationship with U.K than Japan

(ii) There is greater volume of trade with the U.K

(iii) Exportation of crude oil to Japan and none to U.K

(iv) Importation of finished goods to Nigeria from Japan than U.K

subject: Geography
q:
The table below gives the climatic data for two different stations A & B, in Nigeria. Use the data to answer the questions that follow 

station Altitude Latitude   J F M A M J J A S O N D
A 360m 13ºN Rainfall (cm) 0.0 0.0 0.0 1.0 4.8 9.1 15.5 24.9 14.5 1.5 1.5 0.0
Temperature (ºC) 24 28 32 33 32 29 27 26 28 31 29 26
B 225m 7ºN Rainfall (cm) 1.0 2.3 8.9 13.7 15.0 18.8 16.0 8.4 17.8 15.5 4.6 1.0
Temperature (ºC) 27 29 29 28 27 26 24 23 25 25 26 27

(i) Identify the climatic belt to which each station belongs
(ii) Outline the main climatic characteristics of each station
(iii) In what ways will the agricultural practices in the two stations differ?
explanation:
(i) Station: A belongs to the tropical continental (or Sudan) climate while station B belongs to the sub-equatorial climate

(ii) Main climatic characteristics of station A:

(a)It has a high temperature throughout the year

(b) The maximum temperature is 33°C while the minimum is 24°C

(c) Annual temperature range is 9°C

(d) Rainfall is seasonal.

Main climatic characteristics of station B:

(a) The temperature is high throughout the year

(b) Maximum temperature is 29°C while the minimum is 23°C

(c) The annual range of temperature is 6°C

(d) It has a double maxima

(iii) Ways in which agricultural practices in the two stations differ include

(a) Rainfall is used throughout the year in station B while irrigation is used in station A

(b) Cereals are mainly cultivated in station Awhile tree crops are cultivated in station B

(C) Animal rearing is common in station A than in station B

(d) Double cropping is done in station Awhile single cropping is done in station B

subject: Geography
q:
(a) On an outline map of Nigeria locate and name
(i) the source areas and the destinations of two named major items of internal trade, indicate by an arrow the direction of movement of each commodity. (ii) one important town in each area

(b) Outline four factors that have favoured the development of internal trade in Nigeria
explanation:
(b) Four factors that have favoured the development of internal trade include:

(i) Wide demand for such goods

(ii) Good transportation network

(iii) Favourable climate for the cultivation of the crops

(iv) Differences in products

subject: Geography
q:
(a) On a sketch map of Nigeria, locate and name
(i) one area important for the mining of each of limestone, tin, crude oil and coal
(ii) one major town in each area

(b) Explain the importance of any one of the minerals to the economy of Nigeria

(c) Outline any four problems facing the mining industry in Nigeria.
explanation:
(b) Importance of crude oil to the economy of Nigeria; 

(i) Crude oil provides essential products like petrol and kerosine

(ii) It provides employment to Nigerians

(iii) It also provides raw materials for industries

(iv) It provides foreign exchange for the nation

(v) Provision of infrastructure like pipe borne water, good roads, electricity, etc

(c) Four problems facing the mining industry in Nigeria include;

(i) Environmental pollution

(ii) It leads to loss of farmlands

(iii) Lack of skilled labour

(iv) Inadequate transport facilities

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Identify any five problems of development in the Niger Delta region

(b) How can the problems be solved?
explanation:
(a) Five problems of development in the Niger Delta region include:

(i) Problem of soil erosion to environment

(ii) Oil pollution and oil spillage are major problems

(iii) High level of salt inhibits agriculture

(iv) There is poor water transportation development

(v) It has a very low population.

(b) Solutions to the problems include:

(i) Floods and erosion should be controlled

(ii) Adequate care should be taken to avoid oil spillage on water

(iii) The land should be reclaimed from marshy areas

(iv) Flyovers should be constructed

(iv) Provision of basic amenities for the people

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Draw a sketch map of Nigeria to show
(i) the home areas of the following people: Fulani, lbibio, Yoruba and Urhobo
(ii) one major town in each home area

(b) Select any two of these peoples and explain the effects of the physical environment on their occupation and types of house
explanation:
(b)(i) YORUBA: The effects of the physical environment and type of house in Yoruba land are:

(i) Yorubas are mainly involved in farming, weaving, carving, etc.
- Their houses are made of mud.
- Thatched houses and modern buildings are also seen in urban areas

(ii) FULANI: The effects of the physical environment and type of house in Fulani are :
(i) Fulanis are mainly involved in cattle rearing, farming and trading
(ii) Their houses are made of mobile tents made of grass and leaves, no permanent shelter or houses

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Outline any five factors responsible for the low density of population in some parts of West Africa

(b) In what four ways can low population density be an advantage?
explanation:
(a) Five factors responsible for the low population in some parts of WestAfrica include:

(i) Historical factors like war and slavery

(ii) Unfavourable climate in Northern part of West Africa

(iii) Problems of pests and diseases

(iv) Presence of relief, e.g eastern highlands

(v) Lack of mineral resources and social amenities

(b)Advantages of low population density

(i) It leads to high standard of living

(ii) Adequate food supply

(iii) Low pressure on social amenities

(iv) Low crime rate

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Describe any five distinguishing features of plantation agriculture in East Africa

(b) Identify five factors which have favoured the practice of plantation agriculture in East Africa
explanation:
(a) Five distinguishing features of plantation agriculture in East Africa are:

(i) It involves the growing of one crop on the same piece of land

(ii) A large area of land in required

(iii) It requires high capital investment

(iv) Cash crops are mainly involved

(v)All farm operations are mechanized

(b) Five factors which have favoured the practice of plantation agriculture in East Africa include:
(i) Favourable climate
(ii) Rich fertile soils
(iii) High demands for crops (market)
(iv) Availability of cheap labour
(v) Good transportation system

subject: Geography
q:
(a) On an outline map of Africa, locate and name
(i) two land-locked countries
(ii) two ports, each in a different country, serving these land-locked countries
(iii) the countries in which these ports are located

(b) What four major problems are facing land-locked countries in Africa?
explanation:
(b) Four major problems facing landlocked countries in Africa are:

(i) There is economic dependence on other countries

(ii) There is loss of revenue due to smuggling

(iii) There is also the problem of political insecurity

(iv) There is high cost of freight rate

subject: Geography
q:
(a) On a map of Africa, locate and name:
(i) three rivers on which hydro-electric power is generated
(ii) the hydroelectric power station on each river

(b) Examine three factors that favour the generation of hydro-electric power in Africa

(c) Of what importance are hydro-electric power projects to Tropical African countries?
explanation:
(b) Three factors that favour the generation of hydro-electric power in Nigeria include:
(i) A good relief and presence of waterfalls
(ii) Abundance of rainfall
(iii) There must be high volume of water in rivers
