subject: Geography
q:
(a) (i) Draw a well labelled diagram to show the internal structure of the earth
(ii) Describe the distinguishing characteristics of any one of the layers shown in your diagram

(b) Outline the importance of either the hydrosphere or the biosphere to man
explanation:
(a)(i) Internal structure of the Earth

(ii) Characteristics of the crust:

(i) It is made up of upper part to form the continent

(ii) It is made up of granite and contains minerals like silicon and aluminum hence called SIAL

(iii) Its density is 2.7

(iv) It also contains the lower part to form the ocean floor

(vi) It is made up of basalt rocks and contains minerals like silicon, iron and magnesium hence called SIMA

(vi) It has a density of 3.0

(b) Importance of hydrosphere to man include:

(i) Hydrosphere provides water for domestic and industrial uses

(ii) It also provides means of transportation

(iii) It provides food, e.g fish

(iv) It can help to generate electricity (H.E.P)

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Highlight the economic importance of oceans to man, giving specific examples

(b) Select any one warm current in the North Atlantic Ocean and describe the factors responsible for its movement
explanation:
(a) Economic importance of oceans to man include:

(i) Oceans serve as a means of transportation and communication

(ii) They serve as sources of food, e.g fish, crayfish, etc.

(iii) Oceans, e.g continental shelf provide minerals like petroleum and natural gas

(iv) Oceans provide employment to many people e.g fishermen, sailors etc

(v) Extraction of both domestic and industrial salts

(b) One warm current in the North Atlantic Ocean is Floride current.

Factors responsible for its movement include:

(i) Planetary winds. These winds by their nature blow across oceans resulting in the movement of water

(ii) The rotation of the earth

(iii) Differences in temperature

(iv) The shape of the land masses

(v) Salinity of the water

subject: Geography
q:
(a)(i) Name the three climatic belts recognised by the Greek system of classification
(ii) What is the basis of this classification?

(b) State one characteristic of each of the recognised belts

(c) What three advantages has the Koppen's classification over the Greek system?
explanation:
(a)(i) The three climatic belts recognised by the Greek system of classification include:
(i) Torid belt
(ii) Temperate belt
(iii) Frigid belt

(iii) The basis of classification is temperature

(b) One characteristic of each of the belts:

(i) Torrid belt: It is found in the tropics with high temperatures throughout the year

(ii) Temperate belt: It has a seasonal contrast in temperature

(iii) The frigid belt has very low temperature throughout the year.

(c) Three advantages of Koppen's classification over the Greek system include:

(i)It is simple to understand

(ii) It is objective

(iii) It is commonly used in teaching at different levels of educational institutions.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Highlight the distinguishing characteristics of the natural vegetation associated with either the cool temperate (eastern margin type) or the tropical continental climate

(b) Explain the sectors that have aided the occurrence of the vegetation type in the area you have selected.
explanation:
(a) Characteristics of natural vegetation associated with the tropical continental climate:

(i) The dominant vegetation cover is grass

(ii) Presence of tall grasses that are fibrous when old

(iii) The trees are deciduous, i.e they sheath, leaves during the dry season to reduce transpiration

(iv) Presence of short scattered trees

(b) Favourable factors for this type of vegetation include:

(i) Low rainfall of about 600-1500cm per annum

(ii) Extremes of temperature which ranges from 21-32°C

(iii) It has an annual temperature range of 11°C

(v) It has high monthly temperature of about 27°C

(v) The vegetation has a single maximum of rainfall

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Describe five causes of flooding

(b) In what four ways can flooding be controlled?
explanation:
(a) Five causes of flooding include:

(i) Excessive rainfall

(ii) Poor drainage system or lack of culverts

(iii) Erection of buildings on watershed

(iv) Inadequate urban planning

(v) Erection of week dams and embankments

(b) Four ways of controlling floods include:

(i) Channelization of flood from city centres

(ii) Public enlightenment on effects of flooding

(ii;) Construction of wider culverts or good drainage system

(iv) Installation of strong dams to act as flood reservoirs

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Describe the significance of minerals as an environmental resource

(b) Highlight three major problems resulting from the exploitation of mineral resources
explanation:
(a) Significance of minerals as environmental resources include:

(i) It provides fuel, e.g petroleum, coal and natural gas

(ii) It provides foreign exchange

(iii) Provision of employment, i.e mining

(iv) Provision of basic amenities where mining is carried out

(v) Provision of raw materials for industries, e.g limestone for cement industries

(vi) Some are valuable minerals used in making necklace, wrist watches e.g gold.

(b) Three major problems resulting from the exploitation of mineral resources include:

(i) Environmental pollutions

(ii) Depletion off natural vegetation

(iii) Mining activities do lead to soil erosion

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Explain four ways in which a fall in birth rate can be beneficial to a country

(b) What four problems can arise from a fall in the birth rate of a country?

(c) In what four ways can the birth rate of such a country be increased?
explanation:
(a) Four benefits of a fall in birth rate include:

(i) High per capita income

(ii) High standard of living

(iii) Adequacy of available social amenities and services for the people

(iv) Abundant food supply for the few people

(b) Four problems resulting from a fall in the birth rate of a country include:

(i) Inadequate skilled and unskilled labour

(ii) Low rate of exploitation of natural resources

(iii) Low rate of economic development

(iv) High dependency ratio, i.e. more of aged people than young ones

(c) Four ways of increasing the birth rate include:

(i) Encouragement of early marriages

(ii) Legislation in favour of abortion

(iii) Provision of benefits for single parents

(iv) Government incentives such as free medical care.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Explain any five functions which urban centres perform for rural areas

(b) What four problems may limit the performance of the functions identified in (a) above
explanation:
(a) Five functions urban centres perform for rural areas include:

(i) Processing of raw materials from rural areas

(ii) Market for products from rural areas

(iii) Administrative centres for rural areas

(iv) Provision of employment for rural migrants

(v) Provision of social services e.g higher education

(b) Four problems that may limit the performance of the above functions include:

(i) Inadequate communication network between urban and rural areas

(ii) Inadequate jobs in the urban centres

(iii) Inadequate capital for processing

(iv) Political discrimination

subject: Geography
q:
(a) In what fours ways is water transportation important in international trade?

(b) Highlight four limitations of water transpiration

(c) How can these limitations be overcome?
explanation:
(a) Four ways in which water transportation is important to international trade

(i) It covers long distance

(ii) It is very cheap

(iii) It can carry bulky goods

(iv) It aids transportation of passengers

(b) Four limitations include:

(i) Water transportation is too slow for passengers and perishable goods

(ii) Presence of waterfalls and cataracts

(iii) Ports may be silted up or frozen

(iv) Problems of sea weeds or water hyacinth

(c) The above limitations can be overcome by

(i) Continuos dredging

(ii) Improved technology

(iii) Continuous clearing of sea weeds

(iv) Construction of canals to bypass falls

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Explain any five factors that should be considered in the location of a cement factory

(b) Describe any three problems facing cement factories in Tropical Africa
explanation:
(a) Five factors for the location of cement factory include:

(i) Presence of raw materials e.g limestone

(ii) Presence of large market

(iii) Source of power e.g H.E.P

(iv) Availability of capital

(v) Adequate transportation networks

(b) Three problems facing cement factories are:

(i) Inadequate capital to procure machinery

(ii) Inadequate spare parts to service machines

(iii) Inadequate transportation network.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Account for the inter-dependence in trade between Nigeria and Britain

(b) What four factors may limit this trade?

(c) State three ways by which the trade between the two countries can be improved
explanation:
(a) Inter-dependence in trade between Nigeria and Britain include:

(i) Differences in physical setting, e.g climate

(ii) Differences in products - agricultural and manufactured goods

(iii) The need to earn foreign exchange

(iv) Colonial linkage

(b) Four factors limiting the trade include:

(i) Inadequate production of goods

(ii) Low demand for products

(iii) Political instability

(iv) High tariffs on goods

(c) Three ways of improving the trade include:

(i) Increased production by both countries

(ii) Membership of the same international economic organisation

(iii) Increased international co-operation between both countries

subject: Geography
q:
(a) On an outline map of Nigeria, locate and name
(i) an inland drainage basin
(ii) a delta
(iii) a man-made lake
(iv) a confluence town

(b) In what four ways can physical factors limit the use of rivers for transportation in Nigeria?

(c) How can these limitations be overcome?
explanation:
(b) Four physical factors limiting the use of rivers for transportation include:

(i) Presence of waterfall and rapids

(ii) Problems of water weed

(iii) Fluctuating volume of water due to seasons

(iv) Short length of rivers

(c) The above problems can be overcome by

(i) Construction of dams

(ii) Continuos clearing of water weeds

(iii) Dredging of rivers

(iv) Construction of canal

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Draw an outline map of Nigeria and on it, show and name
(i) the savanna belts and one important town in each of the belts

(b) Highlight three vegetation characteristics of any one of the savanna belts shown on your map

(c) Describe the relationship between vegetation and rainfall in the savanna belts. Give reasons for any three of the following in Nigeria
explanation:
(b) Characteristics of Guinea Savanna; 

(i) Presence of tall trees

(ii) Presence of tall grasses

(iii) Trees shed their leaves during the dry season

(iv) Trees found include locust beans, shea-butter 

(c) Relationship between the rainfall and vegetation in the Savanna include:

(i) Rainfall decreases from the Guine2 Savanna towards the Northern limit

(ii) Rainfall duration is longer in the Guinea Savanna than the Sahel Savanna

(iii) Little rainfall of between 20cm - 7cm in the Sahel leads to stunted growth of trees

(iv) Arrival of rainfall marks the beginning of the grasses in the savanna.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Market gardening is predominantly practised around major urban areas

(b) Lumbering is practised mainly in the high forest belt

(c) Rice is cultivated in large quantity in the Middle Belt

(d) Cattle rearing is important in the Sudan Savanna Belt.
explanation:
(a) Reasons for market gardening in urban areas include:

(i) Produce are in high demand - such as vegetable, fruits, etc.

(ii) The need to be close to a large market

(iii) It does not need a large expanse of land

(iv) Presence of good network of routes

(b) Lumbering in the high forest belts:

(i) Presence of suitable climatic conditions-high rainfall throughout the year

(ii) Presence of forest trees

(iii) Presence of varieties of species

(iv) Presence of hardwood for construction purposes

(c) Rice in middle belt; 

(i) Presence of large expanse of land due to low population

(ii) Presence of adequate climatic conditions, e.g rainfall of over 125cm

(iii) Abundance of swampy areas along river valley

(iv) Absence of export crops.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) On an outline map of Nigeria, locate and name the; 
(i) North-Central Highlands, Western Highlands, Adamawa Highlands and Udi Hills
(ii) Rivers Sokoto, Gongola, Oshun and Anambra

(b) Describe four ways in which the North-Central Highlands are of economic importance

(c) Highlight any four problems limiting the economic development of the region
explanation:
(b) Four economic importance of North-Central Highlands include

(i) Presence of mineral deposits e.g tin and columbite

(ii) Rearing of cattle

(iii) Source of many rivers

(iv) Provision of hard rocks for construction

(c) Four problems limiting the economic development of the region include:

(i) Presence of rugged terrain

(ii) Inadequate rainfall

(iii) Soil erosion problem

(iv) Scattered settlements

subject: Geography
q:

(i) Increased trade between member countries 
(ii) Free movement of goods, services, and people 
(iii) Promote cooperation and development in economic activities

(c) Three factors limiting the attainment of the objectives include:

(ii) Strong colonial ties
(iii) Similarity of economic activities.

explanation:
(b) Three importance of river basins include:
(i) It provides good sites for settlement
(ii) It is suitable for agricultural purposes
(iii) Presence of rivers serves as communication routes

subject: Geography
q:
Describe how each of the following factors affects the climate of Africa

(a) Latitude (b) Ocean currents (c) Distance from the sea
explanation:
(a) Effects of latitude on climate of Africa

(i) Insolation if more direct at the equator and included at the tropics

(ii) Rainfall amount is higher at the low latitudes than at the high latitude

(iii) There is rainfall all the year round at the low latitudes and seasonal in high latitudes

(b) Effects of ocean currents

(i) It determines the amount of rainfall
(ii) It affects rain formation
(iii) It leads to coastal fogs
(iv) It modifies temperature 

(c) Distance from the sea

(i) Extremes of temperature in the interior
(ii) It is higher in the interior
(iii) Higher humidity along the coast
(iv) It also leads to thicker cloud cover along the coast

(c) Four problems of plantation agriculture include:

(i) Foreign ownership of farms

(ii) Infestation of pests and diseases

(iii) Fluctuation in prices of produce

(iv) Poor transportation network

subject: Geography
q: Highlight the similarities and differences between latitudes and longitude
explanation:
(a) Similarities of Latitude and Longitudes

(i) Both are imaginary lines

(ii) Both are drawn on the globe or map

(iii) Both are measured in degrees

(iv) Both are used for location of places or positions on the globe.

(v) Both are circular in shape

(b) Differences between Latitudes and Longitudes; 

(i) Latitudes are drawn from West to East, while longitudes a drawn from North to South

(ii) Latitudes are called parallels while longitudes are called meridians

(iii) Longitudes are used to calculate time, while latitudes are not

(iv) Only the equator is a Great Circle among the latitudes while all lines of longitudes form Great Circles with their opposite members

(v) Latitudes are parallel and never meet while longitudes converge and meet at the poles.

subject: Geography
q:
With the aid of diagrams, describe the characteristic and mode of formation of any two of the following:

(a) Rock pedestal (b) Yardang (c) Mesa
explanation:
(a) ROCK PEDESTALS:
Characteristics:

  • It has soft and hard rock layers, mushroom with irregular shape formed in desert e.g Sahara desert
  • The soft rock layers is eroded more rapidly resulting in the formation of irregular edge of alternate bands of hard and soft rock.
  • Further erosion at the base produces a mushroom-shaped feature .

(ii) YANDANG: This is an erosional  feature formed by wind abrasion in  desert e.g Atacama desert. It has vertical bands or hard and soft rocks  of height between 5-15cm.
Characteristics:

  • Yandang is found in the desert.
  • Vertical bands of hard and soft rocks standing in the direction of prevailing wind is eroded such that Rock the softer rock is eroded faster than the hard rock resulting in the formation of Yandang.

q:
Use the climatic data below to answer the questions that follow; 

Month jan Feb Mar Apr May June July Aug Sept Oct Nov Dec
Temp(ºC) - 19 -17 -8 2 10 17 18 18 11 4 -5 -13  
Precipitation (mm) 22 17 29 35 50 78 78 55 55 35 27 22

(a) Calculate the
(i) mean annual temperature
(ii) annual range of temperature
(iii) total annual temperature

(b) Briefly describe the distribution of the precipitation for the station

explanation:
(a)(i) Mean annual temperature = Total temperature (Jan-Dec)
                                                                      12

=  -19 + -17 +........  +-5+-13
                     12                         = 1.5°C

 (ii) Annual range of temperature = temp of hottest month - temp of coldest month
                                18- (-19) = 18 + 19 = 37°C

(iii) Total annual precipitation = Add total precipitation (Jan - Dec) = 27 + 17 +.......  + 27 + 22 = 503mm

 (b) The station has single peak (June and July) and lowest precipitation is in February Total annual precipitation is 503mm. Rainfall from April to October and precipitation is throughout the year

(c) The type of natural vegetation is the cost temperate grassland (or steppe)
Reasons:

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Draw an annotated diagram to show the relief of the ocean basin

(b) Describe the characteristics of any two of the relief features shown on your diagram
explanation:
(a)

 Features to be identified include:

(i) Continental shelf

(ii) Continental slope

(iii) Ocean deep

(iv) Continental edge Land

 (v) Minor trench

(vi) Ocean ridge

(b)(i) Continental shelf:   It has shallow, gentle slope with gradient of 1/500 and it is a continuation of the land to the sea. It occupies about 7% of the total area of the oceans. It is the richest fishing ground, allows ships to move inland and also allows  recreational facilities e.g New found land

(ii) Continental slope: It is steep of depth up to 1801 5401 metres. It joins the continental shelf with the deep sea plain. It is located near the continent.

subject: Geography
q:
Distinguish between the tropical grassland and temperate grassland under the following headings

(a) Location (b) Climate (c) Vegetation characteristics
explanation:
(a) Location: Tropical grasslands are located in the tropics while temperate grasslands are located in temperate latitude. Again tropical grasslands are found between 10° and 30° North and South while temperate grassland are found between 30° and 40° North and South of the Equator

(b) Climate: Tropical grassland has moderate rainfall (25-150cm) while temperate grassland has lower rainfall (40-50cm) Tropical grassland is not all the year round but temperate grassland has cold winter and warm summers. In tropical grassland, summer temperature is 32°C and 21°C while temperate grassland has 18°C in summer and —19°C in winter.

(c) Characteristics: Tropical grassland contains scattered trees while temperate grassland is almost treeless. Trees in the tropical grassland are bigger than those of temperate-grassland.jropical grasses are tough while temperate grasses are succulent. Example of tropical grass is elephant grass while temperate grass is willow.

subject: Geography
q:

(b) Highlight four advantages and four disadvantages of man's intervention with the natural environment
explanation:
(a) The natural environment is the habitat where plants and animals dwell in undisturbed form or state

(b) Four advantages of man's intervention with natural environment include:

(i)Agricultural products for domestic and industrial uses

(ii) Mineral products for industrial uses

(iii) Land for settlement

(iv) Water for domestic and industrial uses.

Four disadvantages for man's interventions with natural environment include:

(i) Depletion of mineral resources

(ii) Depletion of vegetation resources

(iii) Pollution of water, land and air

(iv) Drought and desertification

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Explain the following terms:
(i) Over-population;
(ii) Migration

(b) Describe any three factors which encourage the occurrence of each term in (a) above within a population group
explanation:
(a)(i) Over-population is a situation where a country has more people than its physical and human resources can support with adequate living standard

(ii) Migration is the movement of people on a seasonal, or permanent basis from their original location to another.

(b)(i) Three factors responsible for over-population include:

(a) Low level of education

(b) The culture of the people/large family size)

(c) High birth rate

(ii) Three factors responsible for migration include; 

(a) Environmental hazards e.g earthquake, floods, drought

(b) Low standard of living -search for greener pastures

(c) Fear of insecurity e.g War, Political instability etc.

subject: Geography
q:

(b) Describe the characteristics of any two of the settlement types identified in (a) above.
explanation:
(a) Three classes of settlements based on their functions include:

(i) Commercial e.g London, New York, Lagos, Accra

(ii) Industrial e.g Lagos, Accra

(iii) Administrative e.g Washington DC, Abuja, London

(b)(i) Characteristics
- Commercial settlement/town
- It is a centre of exchange for goods
- It is usually very accessible
- it has high population concentration majority of whom are traders

(ii) Administrative settlement/ town
- it is centre of administration
- It has high population whose population are mainly civil servants
- Presence of social amenities.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Give three reasons why air transportation may be preferred to the other modes of transportation

(b) State any three disadvantages of air transportation

(c) How can these disadvantages be overcome?
explanation:
(a) Three reasons for preferring air transportation includes:

(i) It is the fastest means of transportation

(ii) It can move mails, passengers and high value cargo

(iii) It can cover long distances

(b) Three disadvantages of air transportation includes

(i) High cost of transportation fares

(ii) Air crashes are usually disastrous as chances of survival are highly limited

(iii) There is problem of air terrorism

(c) Solutions to the problems;

(i) Government subsidy of airfares

(ii) Training of flight and technical crew

(iii) Security to be more enforced.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Explain any four problems facing Agriculture in Nigeria

(b) Highlight three effects of these problems on the economic development of Nigeria

(c) How can the problems in (a) above be solved?
explanation:
(a) Four problems facing Agriculture in Nigeria include:

(i) Inadequate labour
- rural depopulation
- drift to the towns/ mines etc leaving old people on the farm

(ii) Land tenure system giving rise to fragmentation of farm holding

(iii) Inadequate capital

(iv) Use of crude implements e.g hoe, cutlass etc.

(b) Three effects of the above problems on the economic development of Nigeria are:

(i) High prices of agricultural products

(ii) Shortage of foods

(ii) Reduction of income to farmers/government

(c) Solutions to the above problems in (a); 

(i) Improvement of rural areas by government

(ii) Introduction of land reforms

(iii) Granting !cans to farmers

(iv) Provision of tractors, plough harrow at reduced price

subject: Geography
q:
Write a geographical account of the Chad basin under the following headings:

(a) Relief and drainage

(b)Agriculture

(c) Problems of development
explanation:
(a) Relief and drainage

(i) The Chad basin is a low-lying area of an average elevation of 45-60m above the sea-level. It is poorly drained and rivers flow into the Lake Chad e.g Hadeija, Gana and Yobe rivers

(b) Agriculture: Farming types include fishing, cattle rearing, irrigation, arable farming, mixed farming etc. Products of agriculture include food crops like groundnut, vegetables, beans, rice, millet, etc while animal products 
include beef, milk, hides and skin, fish etc

(c) problems of development include: Drought, desert encroachment, inadequate natural resources, soil erosion, inadequate capital and low level of technology.

explanation:
Eastern scarpland Western Highlands

(i) Scarplands with average elevation of about 300m above the sea level

- Curved ridges higher than Eastern Scarpland average elevation between 300-600m above the sea level.

(b) People and population:
(i) Tribes are Igbo, Igala
(ii) Population density of over 200 persons/sq km
(iii) Simple dressing 

Largely Yoruba Over 200 persons per sq km Voluminous way of dressing 

(c) Settlement patterns; 
(i) Mainly rural
(ii) Nucleated
(iii) Few linear settlement 

Highly urbanised Nucleated More linear settlement 

(d) Agriculture;
(i) Food crops include yam, coco-yarn, cassava maize, rice
(ii) Cash crops, e.g Oil-palms, citrus, cashew nuts
(iii) Livestock farming -goatS, sheep, Ndama cattle 

Casava, rice, yam, maize, plantain Cocoa, rubber, citrus, coffee, oil palm, kola nuts Coat, chicken, sheep, Muturu cattle

subject: Geography
q:
Using relevant sketches, distinguish between

(a) solstice and equinox

B. the earth's rotation and revolution

C. parallels of latitude and meridians of longitude
explanation:
(a) Equinox and Solstice:

(i) Equinox means equal days and equal nights while solstice means longer days in summer and longer nights in winter

(ii) Equinox occurs on March 21st and September 23rd while solstice occurs on June 21st and December 22nd

(iii) Equinox could be spring or autumn while solstice could be summer or winter

(b) Rotation and Revolution:

(i) Rotation rotation the earth rotates 360° in 24 hours (one day) while it revolve' round the sun in 3651/4 days (one year)

(ii) Rotation causes day night while revolution causes seasons

(iii) The earth rotates on axis while it revolves round the sun along its orbit

(c) Latitude and Longitude:
Latitude runs parallel to the equator while longitude are perpendicular to the equator

(iii) Circumference of latitudes decreases to the poles while longitudes remain constant

(iii) Longitudes measure up to 360° while latitude measure up to 18

(iv) Only the equator is a great circle while two opposing lines c longitude make a great circle

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Discuss, using specific examples, any four factors that determine the pattern of movement of ocean currents the North Pacific Ocean

(b) Select any one cold current in the North Pacific Ocean and outline its effects on thE climate of the adjacent coastland
explanation:
(a)(i) The Planetary Winds: The westerly winds drag the North Pacific Drift from west to east

(ii) salinity from the area of greater percentage of salinity the water flows to the areas of lowest salinity hence North Equatorial current flows northwards

(iii) Earth's Rotation: As the earth rotates from west to east, it carries the body of water along e.g California current to North Equatorial to Kurosho to North Pacific Drift

(iv) Temperature Difference: The water from higher temperature area flows to areas with lower temperature e.g from Equatorial regions to colder region or the Kuroshio or the North Pacific Drift flows towards the north.

(b)(i) One cold current in the north pacific ocean in California or Kamchatka current

(ii) Effects of the California current

(i) Reduction in temperature

(ii) Little or no rainfall

(iii) Development of coastal fog

subject: Geography
q:
4. With the aid of suitable diagrams describe the appearance and process of formation of any two of the following landforms

(a) Rock pedestal (b) Depression (c) Barchan (d) Inselberg
explanation:
Appearance; It is a pillar structure of irregular surface with a minimum shape commonly found in deserts e.g Sahara desert.  

Formation; Alternate beds of horizontal layers of hard and soft rock lying at right angle to the direction of the prevailing wind. It is a sand blasting caused by wind erosion.


Appearance: It is an isolated rocky out crop, with steep sides and round or flat top commonly found in semi arid or arid regions. It may rise up to a height of over 500 metres.

Formation; It is formed from an extensive old plateau. It is shaped by weathering and removal of weathered materials by water and wind

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Outline any four factors that can influence climate of a place

(b) In what three ways does climate affect man
explanation:
(a) Four factors that can influence the climate of a place are:
(i) Latitude
(ii) Altitude
(iii) Continentality
(iv) Ocean currents 

(b) Three ways in which climate can affect man include:

(i) Health;  Humid or damp environment encourages the growth of micro-organisms which cause the diseases of man

(ii) Clothing;. The type and nature of clothing worn by different people in different locations is due to differences in climate. Cold climate requires black and thick dresses while hot climate requires light dresses.

(iii) Building types The type and nature of houses built in different locations is also influenced by the differences in climate e.g hot regions require air-conditioned houses whereas polar regions do not.

subject: Geography
q:
Describe desert encroachment under the following headings

(a) causes (b) areas liable (c) effect (d) methods of prevention
explanation:
(a) Causes of desert encroachment;

(i) Prolonged drought

(ii) Over grazing

(iii) Deforestation or uncontrolled tree felling

(iv) Bush burning

(b) Areas liable:

(I) Desert margins e.g sahel regions

(ii) Sudan Savannah, e.g Sokoto, Maiducuri

(iii) Extreme northern part of Nigeria

(c) Effects; 

(i) Loss of vegetation

(ii) Changes in climate of the areas

(iii) Loss of agricultural land

(iv) Loss of animals and humans by death

(d) Methods of preservation:

(i) Afforestation i.e planting of trees

(ii) Controlled grazing

(iii) Controlled tree felling/deforestation

(iv) Controlled or legislation against bush burning

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Using specific examples, describe any three causes of air pollution

(b) Outline three major effects of air pollution 

(c) Suggest any three ways of controlling air pollution
explanation:
(a) Causes of air pollution

(i) Industrial wastes and fumes

(ii) Fumes from exhaust of vehicles and aero planes

(iii) Fumes from minerals works

(iv) Emission of smoke to the atmosphere

(b) Effects of Air Pollution

(i) It may increase or decrease temperature

(ii) Destruction of the ozone layer

(iii) It causes health hazards, e.g lung diseases

(iv) It may cause accident as a result of poor visibility

(c) Ways of controlling air pollution

(i) Education on waste disposal

(ii) Monitoring of air pollution

(iii) Improved technology, e.g waste recycling

(iv) Government legislation on air pollution

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Explain any four factors that-determine population distribution

(b) Describe any two types of population movement

(c) What four problems are associated with any one of the types of population movement described in (b)
explanation:
(a) Four factors that determine population distribution are; 

(i) Climate: temperature extremes, i.e too high and too low temperature lead to sparse population while moderate temperature leads to dense population

(ii) Relief; Low relief leads to dense population while rugged relief leads to sparse population

(iii) Minerals: Presence of minerals leads to high population while absence of minerals leads to low population

(iv) Soil: Fertile soil attracts dense population while poor soils attract low population

(b) Two types of population movement are:

(i) Rural-Urban Movement

(ii) Urban-Rural Movement

(c) Four problems of Rural-Urban migration

(i) Population pressure on land

(ii) Congestion (traffic, housing)

(iii) Increase in crime wave

(iv) Unemployment

q:
(a) In what four ways does a town differ from a village?

(b) Describe with examples three functions which a town may perform for a village
explanation:
Town Village

(i) It occupies larger area
(ii) High population (over 2,000 persons)
(iii) Population is heterogeneous
(iv) Town is well planned 

- It occupies smaller area
- It has low population (less than 2,000 persons)
- Population is homogenous
- Village is not properly planned 

(b) Three functions which a town may perform for a village include:

(i) Town has industries which can process raw materials provided by the village

(ii) Town also provides employment for people from the village

(iii) The town also provides specialised services like engineering, education, banking insurance for the village.

subject: Geography
q:
Using specific examples, discuss rail transportation in African countries under the following headings

(a) Reasons for construction (b) Problems (c) Solutions
explanation:
(a) Reasons for construction of rail transportation in African countries include:

(i) To open up land, good for major crop production e.g Jos, Maiduguri

(ii) To tap resources in Agriculture, mineral, and forest, e.g rail network in Enugu, Jos, Ibadan

(iii) To link important cities and facilitate movement of people e.g Abidjan-Ouagadougou

(iv) To link different regions thus promoting inter-regional trade e.g North and South of Nigeria

(b) Problems of rail transportation include:

(i) Slow speed

(ii) Poor time schedule

(iii) High cost of spare parts and inadequate capital

(iv) Poor management

(iv) Poor standard of coaches

(c) Solutions:

(i) Need for wider gauge and faster coaches

(ii) Improved management

(iii) Construction of double tracks

(iv) Provision of loans

(v) Availability of market

(c) Three problems facing manufacturing industries are:

(i) inadequate capital

(ii) erratic power supply

(iii) inadequate transportation

(iii) one confluence town
(iv) Bacita and Numan

(b) Describe the commercial production of sugarcane in the Niger-Benue Trough under the following headings:
(i) method of cultivation
(ii) method of harvesting
(iii) economic importance
explanation:
(b) Commercial production of sugarcane under

(i) Method of Cultivation: Land preparation is done and small stalks of sugarcane planted. Planting is done around mid-November-April. Water from Niger and Benue are used for irrigation

(ii) Method of Harvesting: Cane stalks are cut few centimetres above the ground. The leaves are later removed. The canes can be cut by machines or cutlasses after which they are carried to market or factory

(iii) Economic Importance:

(i) As raw materials for sugar industries

(ii) Yields revenue to farmers

(iii) Sugarcane is a food item

(iv) It an serve as fodder or cattle

(iii) two towns important for lumbering within the forest belt.

(b) Describe any four factors which have favoured the production of timber in Nigeria

(c) Outline any four problems facing the lumbering industry in Nigeria
explanation:
(b) Four factors which have favoured the production of timber in Nigeria include:

(i) Heavy rainfall well distributed throughout the year

(ii) Uniformly high temperatures

(iii) Presence of economic species e.g Iroko, Obeche, etc

(iv) High demand for tropical hardwood

(c) Four problems facing lumbering industry in Nigeria are:

(i) Poor road condition during rainy season

(ii) Depletion of economic trees

(iii) Low level of technology

(iv) Species are not in pure stands

subject: Geography
q:
Describe the distinguishing characteristics of either the Western Highlands or the Eastern Highlands under the following heading:

(a) Relief and drainage (b) Agriculture (c) Problems of development
explanation:
Western Highlands:
(a) Relief and drainage: Western highland lies between 300-600m. its hottest summit is Idanre Hill. It serves as source for rivers flowing south and north e.g Osun, Ogun, Ose

(b) Agriculture; Agriculture is mainly subsistence; the region a)so produces grains e.g maize, rice, and root crops like yams, cassava etc. Plantation agriculture is also practised, such as cocoa, kolanut, palm produce and livestock farming

(c) Problems of development
(i) Seasonal water supply
(ii) Presence of pests and diseases
(iii) Problems of erosion
(iv) Problems of floods

(c) State four problems arising from the exploitation of crude oil in Nigeria
explanation:
(b) Three ways in which oil industry has contributed to the economic development of Nigeria are:

(i) Provision of foreign exchange

(ii) Provision of fuel for domestic and industrial use

(iii) Provision of employment opportunities

(c) Four problems arising from the exploitation of crude oil in Nigeria are:

(i) It causes pollution or oil spillage

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Highlight any five features of rotational bush fallowing as practised in WestAfrica

(b) State four advantages of this system of agriculture

(c) Outline any four disadvantages of the system
explanation:
(a) Five features of rotational bush fallowing are;

 (i) Farmlands are left to fallow after one or two years of cultivation

(ii) Farmers return to the farmland after fallow period of 2-5 years

(ii) It uses simple farm tools It uses family labour

(v) Mixed cropping is usually practised

(b) Four advantages of the system include:

(i) Soil fertility is regained naturally

(ii) It is easy to practice as low technology is required

(iii) Fallow area provides good grazing grounds

(iv) It is cheap as little is spent on labour and other inputs

(c) Four advantages of the system include

(i) It wastes land due to large area required

(ii) It leads to land fragmentation

(iii) It leads to soil erosion when soil is exhausted

(iv) It does not give room for mechanization

explanation:
(b) Three effects of mountains on the climate of Africa

(i) It lowers the mean annual temperature, e.g East African highlands

(ii) It leads to heavier rainfall on the wind-ward side of highlands, e.g East of Drakensberg

(iii) It leads to scanty rainfall on the leeward side of mountains

(c) Four ways in which the highlands of Africa are of economic importance are:

(i) They provide a rich soil for agriculture

(ii) They serve as a source of minerals

(iii) They can serve to generate H.E.P

(iv) They serve as tourist centres

explanation:
(b) Three major characteristics of Equatorial Climate:

(i) It has convectional type of rainfall

(ii) It has a double maxima of rainfall

(iii) Temperature is generally high (not less than 27°C)

(c) Three adaptation of vegetation to Equatorial climate include:

(ii) Provision of fruit or food

(iv) Provides raw materials for local craft industries.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) What is the shape of the earth?

(b) With the aid of appropriate diagrams, give four proofs to support your answer in (a) above
explanation:
(a) The earth is spherical

(b) proofs:

(i) sunrise and sunset: The earth rotates from west to east which means t places in the east see the sun before places in the west. If the earth was that flat, all places would see the sun the same time. 

(ii) A Ship's Visibility: The diagram below shows two ships but you can see that the observer is only able to see one of them. If the earth was flat, the observer would see both ships 

(iii) Eclipse of the Moon: When there is an eclipse the moon, the shadow of the earth which is thrown the moon is always round. Only a sphere can ca shadow which is circular

(iv) Circumnavigation of Earth; The earth has been circumnavigated sever times by land, sea and air

subject: Geography
q:
With the aid of diagrams describe the characteristics and mode of formation of any two of the following

(a) Yardang (b) Crater (c) Block mountain
explanation:
Yardang;

 Characteristics:

(i) Found in desert

(ii) Hard and soft rocks are in vertical bands

(iii) A ridge and furrow landscape

(iv) Common in Atacama Desert, Chile and Central Asia

(v) Aligned in the direction of the prevailing wind

(vi) Height between 5 and 15 metres.

Formation: Band of hard and soft rocks which lie parallel to the prevailing winds in the desert are turned into another type of ridge and furrow landscape by wind and abrasion

(b) Block Mountains 


Characteristics:

(i) Found in areas of ancient fold mountains

(ii) Associated with rift valleys

(iii) Uplifted area in the block mountain or horst

(iv) Steepsided

(v) Generally flat topped

(vi) Examples are Ruwenzori in East Africa, Vosges Black Forest in France.

Formation: When parallel vertical faults develop in the crust, they divide the land into blocks. When tensional force sets on the blocks and presses the outside block down while the middle block stands, a block mountain is formed

subject: Geography
q:
Write explanatory notes on any two of the following; 

(a) Flood plain (b) Hydrological cycle (c) River erosion
explanation:
(a) Food plains: It is found at the lower course of a river and generally low lying. During floods, large quantities of materials are transported the upper course of the river and these materials are spread over low lying adjacent areas. 4. layer of sediments gradually builds up the flood plain usually very fertile. It is dangerous for human habitation. Examples are Ganges Plain, Mississippi, Hwang-Ho etc

(b) Hydrological Cycle: This is the movement of water through a series of processes - evaporation, transportation and precipitation. When it rains, some water immediately evaporate back to the atmosphere, while some are absorbed by plants and gradually return to the atmosphere by way of transpiration. Some water run off the slopes and rivers and flow back to seas and oceans while some evaporate directly from the ground to the atmosphere. Clouds are also formed, then rains are formed in the atmosphere and water sent back to the ground and some water percolate in the ground to form ground water.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Explain any four processes of soil formation

(b) In what four ways is soil important?
explanation:
(a) Processes of Soil Formation: Soil formation takes place over a long period of time and it involves the mixture of inorganic mineral water, organic water, liquid substances, gaseous substances, and living organisms. It begins with the production of inorganic mineral matter from parent materials and also involves accumulation or organic substances. Other processes include movement of weathered products in the form of clay particles, colloids or solutions and plant nutrients through soil profile as well as action of living organisms

(b) Importance of Soil; 

(i) Food for micro-organisms

(ii) Medium for plant growth

(iii) Source of materials

(iv) Support for plants

(v) Habitat for macro and micro organisms

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Explain the term atmospheric resources

(b) With specific examples, outline any six uses of atmospheric resource
explanation:
(a) Atmospheric resources: Are those elements such as wind, water, sunlight and gases which are of benefit to man, animals and plants

(b) Specific Examples
(1) Oxygen:

(i) Man breathes in oxygen

(ii) Oxygen combines with water vapour (oxidation) for chemical weathering

(iii) Supports animal life

(iv) Supports combustion

(v) For industrial purposes

(2) Carbon dioxide:
(i) Green plants take in carbon dioxide

(ii) Carbon dioxide and sunlight aid photosynthesis

(iii) Absorbs heat to control atmospheric temperature

(iv) Carbon dioxide combines with water to produce chemical weathering

(3) Nitrogen:
(i) Nitrogen in the atmosphere can be converted to nitrate in the soil

(ii) Combines with water to produce food for plants

(iii) May be used in electrical industries

(4) Water Vapour:
(i) Water vapour moderates atmospheric temperature

(ii) Important for rain formation

(iii) Resultant rain used by plants and animals effectively in the process of chemical weathering

(5) Sunlight:
(i) Provision of vitamin D

(ii) Important for drying

(iii) For photosynthesis

(iv) For solar energy

(6) Wind:
(i) For drying

(ii) For sporting (yatching)

(iii) For winnowing

(iv) For sailing

(7) Ozone: Protects the earth from ultra-violet rays of the sun.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) In what four ways is air transportation different from water transportation?

(b) Highlight three major contributions of transportation to national development

(c) State any three problems of water transportation in Tropical Africa
explanation:
(a) Ways in which air transportation is different from water transportation

(i) Air transportation is faster than water transportation

(ii) Water transport is more suitable for carrying bulky goods than air transport

(iii) Air transport can be used to reach the most remote, most distant and most inhospitable places on earth (in terms of climate) water transport is limited to water bodies only

(iv) water transport is interrupted by hazards of rapids, falls and fogs while air transport is disrupted by visibility alone

(v) Air transport costs more than water transportation in the hauling of goods and services

(vi) Air transport is used for movement of human beings, perishable and precious goods ti water transport.

(b) Major contributions of transportation to national development:
(i) Enhances communication
(ii) Promotes trade
(iii) Generates employment
(iv) Generates revenue
(v) Income for workers
(vi) Promotes tour
(vii) Enhances military movement

(c) problems of water transportation in tropical Africa:

(i) Capital intensive dredging of harbours and waterways

(ii) Seasonal fluctuation of water level

(iii) Presence of water-weeds

(iv) Presence of rapids and falls

(v) Pilfering of goods at seaports

(vi) Sand spits and sand bars

(vii) Inadequate goy to carry at some seaports.

subject: Geography
q:
Differentiate between the practice of agriculture in the sorest and savanna regions of Nigeria under the following headings:

(a) Favourable factors

(b) Products

(c) Economic importance
explanation:
(a) Favourable Factors:

(i) Land areas in the savanna are more suitable for mechanized farming than in the forest region

(ii) There is all year-round rainfall for perennial agriculture in the forest region while seasonal rainfall in the savanna supports seasonal cropping

(iii) Rainfall amount in the forest regions supports trees and root crops, while in the savanna, it supports cereals and legumes

(iv)Animal farming practised more in the savanna

(v) Enough sunshine available in the savanna for fruit ripening while little is required in the forest region

(vi) Two planting seasons in the forest region but one in the savanna region

(vii) Deep well-drained forest soil are suitable for tree and root crops while the shallow but well-drained savana soils favour cereal and legumes

(b) Crops and Animals

(i) Food crops of the savanna include maize, rice, millet sorghum, groundnut, cow-peas, etc, while food crops of the forest region include yams, cassava, cocoyams, kolanuts, coconuts, and various other types of fruits,

(ii) More fruits in the forest

(iii) Savanna is more favourable for large scale livestock farming than the forest region

(iv) More vegetables in the savanna

(v) Cash crops in the savanna include cotton, ginger, groundnuts, while in the forest region they include oil palm, rubber, cocoa and cashew

(c) Economic Importance:

(i) Income to farmers

(ii) Revenue to government

(iii) Industrial raw materials

(iv) Provision of food

(v) Foreign exchange earner

(vi) Employment generation

(iii) More intensive use of land on the Nile than on the Niger basin

(ii) Occasional flooding

(iii) Occurrence of sand spits and bars

(iv) Fluctuation in volumes

(v) Silting at the mouths

(vi) Occurrence of water weeds

(vii) Load carried in suspension

subject: Geography
q:
(a) With the aid of suitable diagrams, describe the appearance and formation of any two of the following landforms 
(i) delta (ii) river capture (iii) waterfall (iv) inselberg

(b) Show one way in which each of the two features in (a) above is of importance to man
explanation:
Appearance: Delta is a feature of lower course of rivers with many distributaries: It is actuate, bird foot or fan-shaped and low lying at the mouth of rivers. Examples are Niger delta, I Main Stream Nile, Mississippi.
Formation: Presence of tideless coast or (River Niger) sheltered coast, sluggish river with heavy deposition of silt at the mouth and availability of lowland areas towards the mouth Tributaries of a river.
Uses: Good agricultural land especially for cultivation' of rice, oil palms and raffia palms. It is also good for fishing and petroleum mining 

Appearance: Two parallel consequent rivers with tributaries separated by a river more powerful and with presence of wind gap. Examples are River irrawady in India, River Ogunpa (Ibadan), in Nigeria.
Formation: One river erodes more aggressively than the other; the more powerful river makes greater rate of headward erosion. The more powerful river cuts through river divide then captures water of weaker stream. The lower part of the pirated stream now forms what is called misfit, the point of capture is termed elbow of capture and a wind gap is formed.
Uses: Rich in agricultural land with the vegetation always available for cattle. The wind gap serves as communication route. 

Appearance: Feature of upper course of river,with steep slope which forms plunge pool. Examples are Kwa fall, Victoria fall, Niagara fall, Erin-ljesa fall, etc
Formation: Resistant rock forms a bar across a river valley, fault line scarp lies across a river course, water plunges down the plateau edge, this may develop to form rapids. Undercutting softer rocks and water fall recedes upstream.

(iv) Inselberg - Appearance: Is found in semi and / arid land form in isolated rocky out crops/composed of granite or gneiss, steep sided/may occur singly or in group round/flat topped-dome shaped. Polished surface/may rise up to over 500 metres with screes found at the base. Examples are Tibesti Plateau, ldanre Hill, Senna hill, Olumo hill, Ahaggar plateau, all in Northern Nigeria and Kalahari desert. Western Australia.
Formation: Formed in semi arid regions, mechanical weathering is dominant and extensive in old plateau(s). Weathering and removal of weathered materials by water and wind with the hard outcrops now exposed.
Uses: Tourism and quarrying

subject: Geography
q:
State with specific examples, how each of the following factors affect(s) the climate of adjacent coastlands:

(a) Winds (b) Ocean currents (c) Distance from the sea
explanation:
(a) Winds: Warm winds raise the temperature of adjacent coastlands, e.g the westerlies bring warm air into Western Europe. Cold winds lower the temperature of adjacent coastlands e.g cold polar winds lower the temperature of the coasts of Newfoundland. Warm, moist winds from the ocean bring rainfall to the adjacent coastland, e.g S.W Monsoon in West Africa. Dry winds from the interior do not produce rain rather they produce dust and dryness, e.g Northeast trade wind brings no rain but dust and dryness to the coastal lands of West Africa. Off shore winds blowing from the interior to the coast produce fogs at the coast, e.g fog formation along the Namibian coast.

(b) Ocean Current: Cold currents lower the temperature of adjacent coastlands e.g the Benguella current, currents help in 
the formation of foes along the coast e.g cold Benguela and Canaries current. Warm currents raise temperature of the adjacent coastlands e.g warm Guinea and warm Mozambican currents. Cold currents result in the formation of coastal deserts e.g Kalahari desert because of cold Benguela current, Sahara desert bec use of cold canaries current

(c) Distance from the sea: Generally the further inland from the sea the less rainfall, e.g Freetown on the coast and Timbuktu inland. Smaller range of temperature along the coast than inland e.g Calabar (less than 30°C) and Agades (more than 15°C) Higher humidity along the coast than further inland generally (one town along the coast and any town inland). Thicker cloud cover along the coast than inland.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) With the aid of annotated diagram, describe the various stages of a river course

(b) Mention four ways in w rivers are of importance to man
explanation:
(a)(i) Upper Course: V-shaped valleys/narrow valleys, swift flowing river, vertical corrosion and river capture. Presence of cataracts, waterfalls, interlocking spurs, plunge pools and gorges

(ii) Middle Course: Wide V-shaped valleys, lateral corrosion and good for transportation. Presence of bluffs, river cliffs, meanders, terraces and some deposition take place

(iii) Lower Course: Lateral corrosion continues with deposition, meander, braiding and levee. Presence of flood plains, increase in volume of water, Ox-bow lakes, deferred confluence, delta and terraces.

(b) Importance of rivers to man:

(i) Transportation of goods and people

(ii) Generation of H. E. P

(iii) Water for domestic use

(iv) Water for industrial use

(v) Water for irrigation

(vi) Fishery

(vii) Sports

(viii) Tourism

(ix) Water for animal use

(x) Natural boundaries

(xi) Flood plains provide rich agricultural land.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Name the three types of rainfall

(b) With the aid of relevant diagrams describe the formation and characteristics of any two types named in (a) above
explanation:
Formation: In areas intensively heated, hot/warm air rises accompanied by rise in relative humidity. Rising air becomes saturated and water vapour condenses with cloud formed quickly and heavy rains accompanied by thunder and lightening.

Characteristics: Short lived, heavy winds, lightening and thunder. Rain galls within limited areas usually in the afternoons. Clear skies immediately after the rain and hailstone may occur. 

Formation: Moist air is forced to rise above a mountain/relief barrier usually on the windward slopes of mountains. Rising air expands and becomes cooler and relative humidity rises and air becomes saturated. Water vapour condenses, cloud is formed with rainfall on the windward slope and descending air on the leeward side
Characteristics: Associated with mountainous regions with even distribution of rainfall over a highland. Windward area has rainfall while leeward side has little or no rainfall and associated with ascending and descending wind.

(iii) Cyclonic Rainfall-Formation: Caused by the meeting of two air masses, the two air masses are different in terms of origin and characteristics. Warm, light, moisture Iadden air rises above cold and dense air. Ascent of the warm air over cold air lowers the temperature of the warm air, cooling takes places and condensation occurs, cloud formation and precipitation follow: 

Characteristics: Associated with fronts and weather system in mild latitudes. Rainfall is convectional types in cold front and it lasts for greater duration than convectional rainfall. It covers extensive area and moves from the West to the East in the northern hemispheres and vice-versa in the south

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Describe the characteristics of the cool temperate grasslands

(b) Outline five factors which affect the distribi of this vegetation type
explanation:
(a) Characteristics of cool temperate grassland:
Grass is luxuriant and succulent for most of the year and the grasslands are treeless plains. In summer, grasses become tough and leaves curled to reduce evaporation. Due to increase in soil moisture few trees may grow along river courses. The appearance of grasses varies with season: green, fresh and blooming in spring but brownish in summer. Typical grasses are buffalo, blue-stern, needle grass with under root system-rhizomes and bulbs. Temperate grassland is called prairies in North America, veldt in South Africa, Pampas in Argentina, Down in Australia

(b) Factors which effect distribution:

(i) Hot summer with temperature exceeding 27°C

(ii) Cold winter with temperate falling below freezing point

(iii) Rich black earth

(iv) Light annual rainfall of about 50cm

(v) Chernozem soil

(vi) Ability of grasses to withstand cold winters by dormancy

(vii) The root system is designed to withstand prolonged drought

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Distinguish between weather and climate

(b) Identify and describe any four elements of weather

(c) Mention three uses of rainfall data to man
explanation:
(a) Weather is the condition of the lower atmosphere of a place over a short period while climate is the average of the weather conditions of a place. The weather of any given place changes from day to day while climate is comparatively constant over a long time

(b) Elements: Temperature, rainfall, wind, humidity, pressure, cloud and sunshine.

Description
(i) Temperature: Heat within the air indicates how hot or cold a place is and varies from latitude to latitude. The sun is the source of all heat and measured with the aid of thermometer. It influences the actual amount of water vapour present in the air and also the rate of evaporation and condensation in the lower atmosphere Temperature decreases with heights

(ii) Pressure: Consists of a number of mixed gases and has weight, exerts weight on the earth surface and this varies from place and from time to time. The higher one goes the lower the temperature and pressure. It is measured with a barometer.

(iii) Cloud: Particles of water droplets in the upper atmosphere suspended in the air. Due to condensation of water vapour carried by rising air, clouds may give precipitation. Cloud reduces visibility and depletes solar insolation. Types include cirrus, cumulus, status, etc.

(iv) Sunshine: Visible part of the solar energy and useful for plants and other living organisms. Determines how dry the wind is and aids the process of formation of clouds and rainfall. The amount of sunshine depends on latitude of a place and the slope of the land affects the amount/intensity of sunshine. Sunshine aspect may affect the amount/ intensity of sunshine. Sunshine varies according to season.

(iv) Rainfall: Water droplets from the atmosphere. Occurs as a result of evaporation from water bodies, rivers, lakes, oceans and transportation from plants. Condensation reaches saturation point, dew point is finally reached. Amount of rainfall is measured with the aid of a language. Types of rainfall are convectional, orographic and cyclonic

(vi) Wind: Air in motion, from high pressure to low pressure belts and has direction. It is made up of series of gusts and eddies, capable of carrying moisture, dust and other pollutants and it adapts to the characteristics of its immediate environment. Measured with wind vane (for direction) while the speed is measured with an anemometer

(vii) Humidity: A measure of dampness of the atmosphere, water in the gaseous state; it varies from place to place at different times of the day. Two types: absolute and relative and it is measured with hygrometer

(c) Uses of rainfall data:

(i) To determine the characteristics of rainfall

(ii) For agricultural purposes

(iii) For calculation of monthly mean rainfall

(iv) For calculation of mean annual rainfall

(v) For planning social activities

(vi) For air and sea navigation purposes

(vii) For planning military operations

(viii) For educational purpose (teaching and research)

(b) Describe the physical changes that are likely to be associated with the increasing reduction of the ozone layer in the upper atmosphere
explanation:
(a) ways in which human activities affect the composition of the atmosphere ;

(i) Emission of carbon dioxide, e.g burning fossil fuel such as coal, petrol gas

(ii) Deforestation

(iii) Use of chemical, biological and nuclear warfare

(iv) Release of toxic waste

(v) Bush burning

(vi) Pollutants emitted from industrial activities

(vii) Pollutants emitted from mining and construction works

(b) physical changes that are likely to be associated with the increasing reduction of the ozone layer in the upper atmosphere 

(i) As the ozone layer increases the amount of ultra-violet rays increases in the atmosphere

(ii) The increase in ultra-violet rays increases the amount of solar radiation at the earth's surface

(iii) Excessive ultra-violet rays leads to increase in skin cancer in man

(iv) Increased temperature leads to melting of polar ice and this leads to rise in sea levels.

(v) Possible increase in precipitation

(vi) Death of chlorophyll in plants leads to inability of plants to manufacture food.

(i) Ever-green plants due to high temperature and rainfall throughout the year

(v) Sahel Savanna:

(iii) Shrubs are thorny to reduce evapotranspiration.

subject: Geography
q:
Using specific examples, describe how each of the following factors affect the vegetation of Africa;

(a) Rainfall (b) Altitude (c) Soil type
explanation:
(a) Rainfall: Areas with heavy rainfall of over 150cm have dense forest. Rainfall throughout the year gives rise to evergreen vegetation, e.g tropical rainforest. Areas with winter, rainfall an dry summer have Mediterranean vegetation. When rainfall is less than 150cm and occurs mainly in summer the vegetation is often grass, e.g savanna and the veldts. Where rainfall is very low, less than 50cm, we have semi-desert and desert vegetation

(b) Altitude: Vertically zoned vegetation types and montane vegetation at high altitudes e.g Kenya Mountain and Cameroon Mountain, Short temperate grassland, dwarfed trees, and lowland forests.

(c) Soil Types: Limestone soil in the Mediterranean regions gives rise to low growing vegetation called garrigue. Clayey soils in areas of high rainfall gives rise to mangrove swamps e.g Niger Delta Areas with loamy soils and good rainfall gives rise to luxuriant vegetation e.g Rainforest belt. Sandy soils gives rise to poor grassland and desert, e.g Sahara desert

(ii) Dry areas (annual rainfall less than 75cm e.g Sokoto and Chad basin

(iv) Low humidity

(v) Development of large scale irrigation schemes e.g Tiga Dam, Kainji Dam

(vi) Presence of fertile alluvial soils e.g Niger basin

(b) Major farming areas and crops:

(i) Niger-Benu a river valley sugarcane, rice maize

(ii) Sokoto-Rima river valley rice, tobacco, cotton sweet potatoes, onion, groundnut

(iii) Chad basin wheat, millet, guinea corn, rice, cotton, onions,

(iv) Jebba and Kainja areas in the middle belt of Nigeria sugarcane rice:

(v) Gongola-Numan River Basin sugarcane rice, guinea corn etc.

(c) Problems:

(i) Flooding

(ii) Fluctuation in level of water

(iii) Displacement of settlements

(iv) Many dams disrupt normal flow of rivers

(iv) Possible accumulation of mineral salt e.g sodium

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Describle any four ways in which lines of longitude are different from lines of latitude

(b) Calculate the approximate distance in a straight line between Tema (Latitude 6°N) and London (Latitude 52°N) (Show all workings clearly)
explanation:
(a)(i) Lines of longitude are imaginary lines running from north to south on the earth's surface while lines of latitude are imaginary lines on the earth's surface running from east to west.

(ii) All lines of latitude are parallel while lines of longitude converge at the poles

(iii) All opposite lines of longitude form great circles while the Equator is the only line of latitude that forms a great circle

(iv) Lines of latitudes are used for calculating distance while lines of longitude are used for calculating local time of places

(v) Lines of latitude decrease in length from the 
equator towards the poles while all lines of longitude are of equal length

(vi) Line of latitude increase in value from the equator towards the poles while lines of longitude increase in value eastwards and westwards of the Greenwich meridian

(vii) The significant lines of latitude are 0°, 23112°N, 231/2°S, 661/2°N, 661/2°S and 90°N and S, while those of longitude are 0° and 180° East and West.

(b) Solution: The average distance of 1° of latitude = 111km. Difference in latitude between Tema and London,
i.e 52° - 6° = 46°, Actual distance = 111 x 46° = 5,106km

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Give two examples each of sedimentary and metamorphic rocks

(b) Outline four major differences between sedimentary and metamorphic rocks
explanation:
Rocks                             Examples 
Sedimentary rocks         Coal, limestone, chalk, gypsum, clay, etc
Metamorphic rocks        Slate, marble, graphite, quartzite, schist, gneiss, etc

(b) The main differences between sedimentary rocks and metamorphic rocks are as follows:

(i) Permeability: Sedimentary rocks are more permeable while metamorphic rocks are more impervious.

(ii) Fossils; The fossils in metamorphic rocks have been destroyed by heat and pressure while sedimentary rocks have beautiful fossils

(iii) Jointing: The sedimentary rocks are more jointed than the metamorphic rocks

(v) Mineralogy: Metamorphic rocks contain non-ferrous minerals while sedimentary rocks contain hydrocarbons as minerals

(vi) Lustres: Metamorphic rocks are crystaline while sedimentary rocks are non-crystaline

(vii) Relief: Sedimentary rocks generally subtend lower relief than metamorphic rocks

(viii) Mode of formation: Metamorphic rocks are formed due to heat and intense pressure while sedimentary rocks are formed through deposition of weathered materials, remains of organic matter and evaporation of chemically charged water. Metamorphic rocks are formed out of existing rocks whereas sedimentary rocks are products of weathering.

subject: Geography
q:
(a)(i) Name two landforms produced by faulting
(ii) Describe the characteristic features of one of the landforms named in (a)(i) above

(b) Highlight the importance of mountains to man
explanation:
(a)(i) The two major landforms produced by faulting are:

(i) Rift valley or graben

(ii) Block mountain or Horst mountain

(a)(ii) The characteristic features of block or horse mountain are as follows:

(i) The presence of a block between two faults

(ii) It has a fairly flat top

(iii) It has steep straight edges

(iv) It is usually very high

(v) The examples can be located in the following areas- Ruwenzori, Black forest, Vosges, Abyssinian highland.

The major features of Rift Valley or Graben are as follows:

(i) The presence of trough between two parallel faults

(ii) It has steep-sided walls

(iii) It is usually very deep

(iv) It is flat, but at times with irregular floor

(v) The examples include - Rhine valley, Dead sea, Jordan valley, Great East Africa Rift Valley

(b) The following are the importance of mountain to man; 

(1) Mountain modifies climate, so ideal for settlement e.g Jos (Nigeria), Elgon, Mt Kilimanjaro, Mt Kenya.

(2) It serves as sources of rivers e.g River Niger from the Guinea Highlands

(3) Tourist attractions - holiday resorts like Olumo rocks, (dare Hills and the Swiss Alps

(4) Rocks from highlands are used for construction e.g rocks from the Shai Hill were used to construct the Tema Harbour in Ghana

(5) Mountain encourages relief, rain formation as seen in Jos (Nigeria) and Cameroun mountain

(6) Minerals are mined from the highlands e.g Tin, Columbite from Jos Plateau, Coal from Udi Hills Gold from the Rockies

(7) Practice of transhumance on mountain pasture in Switzerland and Austria

(8) It serves as site for communication equipment e.g TV Antenna, Radio mast

(9) Mountains serve as natural boundaries e.g Mt Cameroun between Nigeria and Cameroun

(10) It encourages terrace farmie (agriculture) as seen in Jos, Biu Plateau and Udi Hills

(11) Heaven against invaders especially in the olden days (defense).

(12) Lumbering in mountain slopes e.g Scandinavia for timber, paper and pulp and matches industries

subject: Geography
q:
(a) With the aid of a well-labelled diagram, describe a typical soil profile

(b) Highlight the importance of soil Use the climatic data in the table below to answer the following questions
explanation:
The description of a soil profile.
A Horizon (Zone of eluviation): It is the surface or top soil. This is the zone where organic materials have become incorporated within the mineral matter. the zone of eluviation has little of fresh plant remains at the surface but below is a layer of very dark humus. Below the humus, is a light coloured mineral layer produced from leaching. This zone supports maximum biological activity of plants or micro-organisms hence a considerate part of the nutrients and water available for plants are found. The most extensive roots developed in this zone support life

B Horizon (Zone of illuviation): This is the subsoil which is lighter in colour because it contains less humus. It also contains little organic material unit which makes the mineral matter weathered Zone of illuviation contains substances derived from the A horizon through leaching and some substance derived from the C horizon through evaporation. Clay is also washed down from the surface. The major mineral compounds that accumulate in this zone include iron, calcium and aluminum.

C Horizon: The C horizon is the regolith composed of the same parent materials. The true soil above developed from it because it is more.or less weathered. This zone is mostly solid hence it is regarded as the reservoir of minerals.

 (b)(i) It is the main medium of plant growth

(ii) It is the habitat for micro-organisms, reptiles and some animals

(iii) It serves as raw materials for industries e.g sand, clay, limestone, etc

(iv) It serves as determinants of the type of agriculture

(v) It serves as support for building

(vi) It contains all the essential minerals

(vii) Soil serves as food for micro-organisms such as earthworm

subject: Geography
q:
(a) List four atmospheric resources

(b) Describe three uses each of any two of the resources listed in (a) above

(b) Name three other environmental resources that are of great importance to man.
explanation:
(a) The four atmospheric resources are
(i) solar energy
(ii) water vapour
(iii) wind
(iv) gases
(v) water
(vi) vegetation

(i) Solar energy: It is used for generating energy when carefully harnessed for the production of electricity. It also provides vitamin 'D' and light. Solar energy is used in drying crops and clothes. It serves as energy for photosynthesis in plants

(ii) Water vapour: It is important for rain formation and makes the atmosphere fresh. Water vapour is useful to man and plants

(iii) Wind: Wind helps to modify the weather: It is responsible for the dispersal of seed and aid drying of clothes and crops. It also aids the formation and distribution of rainfall. The harnessed wind mills aid winnowing process of cereals. Wind aids sailing boats.

(iv) Gases: The two major gases that are of immense importance are oxygen and nitrogen

(i) Oxygen: Oxygen supports animal life and aids breathing in man. It also supports combustion

(ii) Nitrogen: Provides food for plant growth. It is useful in the process of soil formation. Nitrogen aids process of photosynthesis and breathing in plants

subject: Geography
q:
(a) State four differences between local craft and heavy manufacturing industries

(b) Outlines three factors that have influenced the location of an iron and steel industry in Tropical Africa

(c) In what three ways has industrialization contributed to the economy of Tropical African countries?
explanation:
(a)(i) Local craft industries produce on small scale (low output) while heavy manufacturing industries produce on large scale (mass production)

(ii) Local craft industries involve few people generally below ten people in the production process while heavy manufacturing industries involve many people

(iii) Simple tools which are normally not powered are used in local craft industries while heavy power-driven machines are used in heavy manufacturing industries

(iv) Local crafts are produced at home or in small workshops while heavy manufacturing industries take place in specially built factories

(v) Local craft industries require little investment whereas heavy manufacturing industries require a lot of capital

(vi) Local craft industries use mainly local raw materials while heavy manufacturing industries depend on both local and imported raw materials.

(vii) Local craft industries involve great skills and artistry but in heavy manufacturing industries, artistry is not necessary.

(b) Factors of location of an iron and steel industry in tropical Africa, e.g Ajaokuta, Kogi State, Nigeria

(i) Proximity to raw materials
(ii) Proximity to power
(iii) Supply of labour
(iv) Transport
(v) Market
(vi) Capital
(vi) Government policy
(vii) Availability of water

(c)(i) Raising of general standard of living

(ii) More foreign exchange from processed commodities

(iii) Transfer or technology

(iv) Development of infrastructure

(v) Provision of employment

(vi) Favourable balance of trade

(vii) Generation of revenue to the government through taxes and royalties

(viii) Expansion of local markets

(ix) Aiding industrial research.

(vi) Water is cheaper in terms of construction and maintenance

(b)(i) Leads to specialization and therefore more production

subject: Geography
q:
Compare the North Central Highlands with the Cross River Plain under the following headings:

(a) Relief and drainage

(b) People and settlement

(c) Economic activities
explanation:
(a) Relief and Drainage:

(i) The North Central Highlands is a highland area while the Cross River Basin is a lowland area

(ii) Average height of North Central Highlands is 750m while the Cross River plains is 120 -180m

(iii) In the two areas, isolated hills and ridges are found

(iv) North Central Highlands has radial drainage pattern while Cross River plains has dendritic patterns of drainage

(v) North Central Highlands is well drained while the Cross River plains has a lot of swampy clay depressions

(b) People and Settlement:

(i) Both areas are inhabited by many ethnic groups

(ii) North Central Highlands have Hausa Fulani, Birom Angas, Kaje, etc while the Cross River plains have lgbo, lbibio, Ekoi, Efik, Ukelle, etc

(iii) In both regions, there are areas of dense population and areas of sparse population

(iv) In Cross River plains settlements are mainly on the interfluve while in North Central Highland settlements are mainly in the plain and foot of the hills

(v) Nucleated and dispersed settlements in both areas

(c) Economic Activities:

(a) In both areas, crop farming is the most important occupation and the food crops include rice, yam, soya beans, guinea corn and maize

(b) In Cross River plains cash crops include oil palm and rubber while for the North Central Highlands they include cotton, groundnut and wheat

(c) The mining is important in North Central Highlands while petroleum mining is important in Cross River plains. Local crafts in North Central Highlands include brass work, leather works and clothes dyeing while in Cross River plains they include cane and raffia work. In Cross River plains people carve on wood but in North Central Highlands they carve on calabash.

subject: Geography
q:
(a) Describe two methods of irrigation agriculture in the Nile Valley

(b) Give three reasons for the development of irrigation agriculture in this region

(c) Highlight four problems associated with this type of agriculture in the Nile Valley
explanation:
(a) Methods of irrigation agriculture in the Nile Valley:

(1) Basin or flooding method involves the seasonal flooding of the nearby lands in the Nile Valley during which the flood water is trapped and cannot flow back into the river

(2) Perennial method involves the draining of rivers to create artificial reservoirs which can supply water to irrigation canals at any time of the year

(3) The shaduf method - oldest method during which farmers collect water from river channel either with a bucket or a water bag, and pour on their farms

(4) Pumping method-lifting of water from the river to the farm. Water is pumped into the canals by electricity

(i) Large areas are thus covered

(ii) This method is quite expensive

(iii) Hence, not commonly used by peasant farmers

(iv) Sagia or sakai/sprinkler method

(v) Animals are used to turn wheels

(vi) Containers/buckets are tied to the wheels

(vii) As the animals turn, the wheel rotates

(viii) Containers/ buckets dip into water

(ix) Water is lifted to the farmland to wet the crops.

(b) Reasons for development of irrigation in the Nile Valley:

(a) To supply water to the soil as little or no rain is experienced around these areas

(b) To fight drought as Egypt experiences desert climate

(c) To produce cash crops for export e.g cotton, sugarcane, wheat etc

(d) To produce crops all the year round

(e) To extend the area under cultivation

(c) Problems associated with irrigation agriculture in the Valley:

(1) There is the problem of fluctuation in the level of water used for irrigation purposes

(2) Pests and diseases

(3) Water borne diseases

(4) Presence of excess of mineral salts like sodium which are injurious to plant growth

(5) Over flooding could be disastrous

(6) Displacement of people

(7) Construction of many dams disturbs normal flow of water

(8) Silting of dam.

subject: Geography
q:
(i) On an outline map of Africa, mark and name
(i) the equatorial climatic zone
(ii) one Mediterranean zone
(iii) one important town in each zone

(b) State four characteristics of the equatorial climate

(c) Describe two ways in which the equatorial climate affects agriculture
explanation:
(b)  characteristics of the equatorial climate;

(a) Heavy rainfall of over 200cm

(b) Rainfall well distributed over the year

(c) Two periods of maximum rainfall

(d) Constant high temperature (around 27°C)

(e) Low daily range of temperature (8°C)

(f) Abundant cloud cover

(g) High humidity (90% in the morning)

(h) Low annual range of temperature (3°C)

(c) ways in which the equatorial climate affects agriculture  

(1) Heavy rainfall supports tree crops such as cocoa, oil palm, rubber

(2) Distribution of rainfall throughout the year gives rise to plantation agriculture

(3) Two very wet periods and two less wet periods result in double cropping

(4) Due to long period of rainfall, root crops that do not mature fast are planted

(5) Heavy rainfall results in rapid growth of weeds

(6) High temperature and heavy rainfall result in pests like tsetse fly, so cattle rearing is hampered

(7) Adverse effects on the use of large scale mechanization.
